proposals.title: Differential SARS-CoV-2 Testing Behavior by Vaccination Status: A Bias Analysis in Cohort Studies of Vaccine Effectiveness Against Medically Attended COVID-19
proposals.motivation: Observational studies of COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness (VE) against medically attended outcomes rely on diagnostic testing. A core, often untested, assumption is that testing propensity is similar between vaccinated and unvaccinated individuals with comparable symptoms. If vaccinated individuals are more or less likely to seek testing, this differential misclassification could bias VE estimates, leading to incorrect conclusions about protection.
proposals.hypothesis: Among patients presenting to healthcare with acute respiratory illness (ARI), vaccinated individuals have a systematically different probability of receiving a SARS-CoV-2 test compared to unvaccinated individuals with similar clinical and demographic profiles, after adjusting for encounter type and severity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record (EHR) data from a large integrated health system (e.g., Kaiser Permanente, Veterans Health Administration). The cohort will include all adult patients with an outpatient, emergency department, or inpatient encounter with an ARI diagnosis code (ICD-10) from a defined period (e.g., Omicron era). We will use multivariable logistic regression to model the probability of receiving a SARS-CoV-2 PCR or antigen test during the encounter. The primary exposure will be mRNA vaccination status (unvaccinated, primary series, boosted), adjusted for covariates including age, comorbidities (e.g., psychiatric disorders from prior work), prior infection, encounter setting, and a propensity score for vaccination to balance unmeasured confounding. We will quantify bias by comparing crude and adjusted odds ratios for the association between vaccination and testing.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence on the validity of a critical assumption in VE research. It will quantify the magnitude and direction of potential bias introduced by differential testing. The findings will inform the design and interpretation of future observational VE studies, suggesting when and how to adjust for testing behavior.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Stenehjem and others has compared protection from vaccination and prior infection, it has not explicitly modeled testing as an intermediate outcome to assess bias. This proposal inverts the standard analytical framework to treat testing not as a perfect capture mechanism but as a potential source of differential misclassification to be measured.

proposals.title: Modeling Waning Vaccine Protection: A Time-Continuous and Biomarker-Informed Approach to COVID-19 Risk in a High-Risk Cohort
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies of COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness, including Dr. Stenehjem's work, often treat vaccination as a binary (yes/no) or categorical (dose count) variable. This approach obscures the continuous nature of waning immunity over time and ignores individual variability in immune response. A more granular model is needed to accurately quantify protection, especially for vulnerable populations like those with psychiatric disorders, to inform optimal booster timing and clinical risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling vaccination status as a continuous function of time since last dose and incorporating serological biomarkers (anti-spike IgG) will significantly improve the prediction of COVID-19-associated hospital encounters compared to traditional binary/categorical models, and will reveal distinct waning patterns across clinical subgroups.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked EHR and vaccination registry data from a large US health system (2021-2023). For each person-day of follow-up, we will compute a time-varying 'protection score' using a penalized spline function of days since last mRNA vaccine dose. We will integrate this with quantitative anti-spike IgG titers from available outpatient lab data. Using Andersen-Gill extended Cox models, we will assess the association between this continuous protection score and the hazard of COVID-19 hospitalization/ED encounters, adjusting for prior infection, comorbidities, and demographics. Subgroup analyses will focus on psychiatric diagnoses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, dynamic framework for estimating real-world vaccine effectiveness that moves beyond static categories. It will deliver quantitative estimates of how protection decays over time and how biomarker levels modify this relationship. The findings will offer a more precise evidence base for public health guidance on booster vaccination schedules, particularly for medically complex patients.
proposals.novelty: While waning is recognized, most pre-2026 epidemiological models do not operationalize vaccination as a continuous, time-decaying exposure variable. The integration of routinely collected serological biomarkers into a large-scale, time-to-event analysis to refine this decay function is a significant methodological advance over existing literature.

proposals.title: Effectiveness of a Single mRNA Vaccine Dose Against COVID-19 Hospitalization and Severe Outcomes in Adults During Omicron Predominance
proposals.motivation: While mRNA vaccine effectiveness (VE) for complete primary series and booster doses is well-established, real-world evidence for partial vaccination (single dose) is limited, especially against Omicron variants. This gap is critical for public health guidance, resource allocation, and understanding the baseline protection in individuals who delay or cannot complete the primary series. Assessing single-dose VE informs strategies for vaccine deployment and patient counseling.
proposals.hypothesis: A single dose of an mRNA COVID-19 vaccine provides measurable but suboptimal protection against severe COVID-19 outcomes (hospitalization, ICU admission, death) compared to unvaccinated individuals, and is significantly less effective than a completed primary series, during periods of Omicron variant predominance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health records from a large US healthcare system (e.g., Intermountain Healthcare, as in Stenehjem's prior work). The primary exposure will be vaccination status: unvaccinated, single-dose (≥14 days post-dose), and complete primary series (2 doses). The primary outcome is COVID-19-associated hospitalization. We will use a Cox proportional hazards model, adjusting for age, sex, comorbidities, prior SARS-CoV-2 infection (documented by PCR), and healthcare encounter days. VE will be calculated as (1 – hazard ratio) * 100%. Subgroup analyses will focus on immunocompromised adults and those with psychiatric disorders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first detailed estimates of single-dose mRNA VE against severe Omicron outcomes in a general adult population, filling a key evidence gap. It will quantify the gradient of protection from zero to one to two doses, offering concrete data for public health messaging on the importance of series completion. Findings will also inform risk assessment for partially vaccinated patients with specific clinical vulnerabilities.
proposals.novelty: Prior studies by this team and others have focused on VE of complete regimens or prior infection; a dedicated, rigorous assessment of the single-dose 'partial schedule' against severe Omicron outcomes is novel. The analysis leverages established methods from the researcher's prior work but applies them to a distinct, policy-relevant exposure group.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Federated, Real-Time Test-Negative Design System for Continuous COVID-19 Vaccine Effectiveness Monitoring in the United States
proposals.motivation: Post-authorization monitoring of COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness (VE) against emerging variants has relied on periodic, labor-intensive studies, creating lags in public health response. A scalable, automated, and near real-time national surveillance system is urgently needed. Federated electronic health record (EHR) networks, like the National Patient-Centered Clinical Research Network (PCORnet), offer a pre-existing infrastructure to enable this but require adaptation for continuous VE estimation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a standardized, federated analytics pipeline can be implemented across a national EHR network to automate the test-negative design, generating timely, reliable estimates of COVID-19 mRNA vaccine effectiveness against hospitalizations and emergency department encounters.
proposals.method: We will leverage the PCORnet infrastructure to implement a distributed, federated analysis. A central Common Data Model (CDM) will define cohorts: test-positive COVID-19 cases and test-negative controls from emergency/urgent care encounters. A pre-specified, versioned analysis code package (using R or Python) will be deployed to each participating site. This package will perform local propensity score matching or multivariable logistic regression, adjusting for covariates like age, comorbidities, prior infection, and calendar time. Sites will return only aggregated, de-identified summary statistics for meta-analysis to estimate pooled VE, preserving patient privacy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, operational framework for continuous, national-level VE surveillance, dramatically reducing the latency from data generation to actionable insight. It will provide a model for rapid assessment of future vaccine formulations and a scalable template for monitoring other preventive interventions against infectious diseases using real-world data.
proposals.novelty: While test-negative design studies exist, automating and scaling this method within a federated EHR network for continuous, real-time estimation represents a significant methodological and operational advance beyond static, retrospective cohort studies conducted pre-2026.

proposals.title: Predictors of COVID-19 mRNA Vaccine Uptake and Timing in a Large U.S. Health System: An Electronic Health Record-Based Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: While extensive research, including Dr. Stenehjem's prior work, has quantified the effectiveness of COVID-19 mRNA vaccines, understanding the drivers of vaccination itself remains a critical public health gap. Identifying patient-level predictors of vaccine uptake and timing is essential for designing targeted interventions to improve coverage, especially among vulnerable populations, as the pandemic transitions to an endemic phase with ongoing booster recommendations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of sociodemographic factors, clinical comorbidities (including psychiatric disorders), healthcare utilization patterns, and prior SARS-CoV-2 infection status will be significant independent predictors of both the likelihood and the speed of initial and subsequent COVID-19 mRNA vaccine uptake.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using the same EHR data sources from Intermountain Healthcare employed in the researcher's prior vaccine effectiveness studies. The cohort will include all adults (≥18 years) with ≥1 encounter from 12/2020. The primary outcome is time-to-initial mRNA vaccine dose. We will employ multivariable Cox proportional hazards models to identify predictors. Secondary analyses will use logistic regression for booster dose uptake and multistate models to assess transitions between unvaccinated, vaccinated, and infected states, adjusting for covariates like age, sex, race/ethnicity, area deprivation index, comorbidities, and prior healthcare encounters.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a detailed, patient-level profile of factors associated with vaccination behavior within a large integrated health system, complementing existing ecological or survey-based data. It will directly inform health system-led outreach and intervention strategies by pinpointing subpopulations with lower uptake. Furthermore, it will establish a methodological framework for using EHR data to study vaccination drivers for other vaccines.
proposals.novelty: This flips the analytical lens of the researcher's established vaccine effectiveness pipeline to model the antecedent behavior—vaccination itself—using identical data and similar causal inference methods. It uniquely integrates detailed clinical history, including psychiatric diagnoses and prior infection status, with healthcare utilization metrics to predict vaccine timing, not just binary receipt.

proposals.title: Vaccine-Induced Immune Pressure and SARS-CoV-2 Spike Protein Evolution: A Single-Center Genomic and Clinical Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Population-level studies have established the effectiveness of mRNA vaccines against COVID-19-associated encounters, but the potential for vaccination to drive localized viral evolution remains poorly characterized. Understanding this dynamic is critical for anticipating variant emergence and informing vaccine deployment strategies, especially in high-risk patient populations frequently encountered in hospital settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a defined patient population, increasing immune pressure from mRNA vaccination, quantified by vaccination status and time since last dose, is associated with the accumulation of non-synonymous mutations in the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, particularly in antigenic regions, among sequenced clinical isolates.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study at a single academic medical center, linking high-throughput viral whole-genome sequencing data from residual clinical specimens (2021-2023) with detailed EHR data on patient vaccination dates, doses, prior infections, and demographics. Using Poisson regression models, we will test for an association between the patient's vaccination exposure (primary series and booster doses, modeled as a time-dependent covariate) and the number of spike protein mutations per isolate, adjusting for viral lineage, patient age, comorbidities, and prior infection. Sequence alignment and mutation calling will be performed using the pre-2026 standard pipeline (i.e., Nextclade).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, granular view of the potential for host vaccination pressure to shape viral evolution at the local level. It will generate a unique, linked genomic-clinical dataset to model evolutionary dynamics and could identify specific immune-escape mutations emerging in a real-world, clinically relevant context.
proposals.novelty: While population-level genomic surveillance is common, this study's novelty lies in its tight integration of individual-level vaccination history and precise timing with viral sequence data from the same patients, moving beyond ecological correlation to test a direct mechanistic hypothesis at the patient-virus interface.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Heterologous mRNA Vaccine Booster Schedules Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters During Omicron Subvariant Predominance
proposals.motivation: Following the widespread deployment of mRNA COVID-19 vaccines (BNT162b2 and mRNA-1273), heterologous boosting (mixing products) became a pragmatic reality due to supply and policy. Real-world evidence on the comparative vaccine effectiveness (VE) of specific mixed schedules versus homologous schedules against Omicron subvariants remains limited. This is critical for informing public health recommendations and understanding immune correlates of protection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that heterologous mRNA booster schedules (e.g., primary series with BNT162b2 followed by mRNA-1273 booster) will provide superior protection against COVID-19-associated emergency department and hospital encounters during Omicron BA.4/BA.5 and subsequent subvariant predominance compared to homologous booster schedules.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked data from a large US healthcare system (e.g., Veterans Affairs, Kaiser Permanente) and immunization registries. The study population will be adults who completed a primary mRNA series and received a first mRNA booster. We will define exposure groups based on specific product sequences (BNT/BNT/BNT, BNT/BNT/Moderna, Moderna/Moderna/Moderna, Moderna/Moderna/BNT). The primary outcome is a COVID-19-associated encounter (ED or hospitalization) with a positive SARS-CoV-2 test. We will use a Cox proportional hazards model to estimate adjusted hazard ratios, adjusting for demographics, comorbidities, prior infection, and healthcare utilization. VE will be calculated as (1 – adjusted hazard ratio) × 100%. We will conduct stratified analyses by immunocompromised status and age.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world estimates of VE for specific mixed mRNA vaccine schedules against clinically relevant Omicron-era outcomes. It will directly inform clinical and public health guidance on optimal booster strategies. Furthermore, it will contribute methodologically to the assessment of heterologous vaccination using rigorous, encounter-based endpoints consistent with the researcher's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have compared homologous vs. heterologous boosting in immunogenicity trials or against earlier variants, this analysis focuses on head-to-head comparisons of specific mixed product sequences against contemporary Omicron subvariants using a clinically meaningful, encounter-based outcome in a large, diverse population.

proposals.title: Integrated Surveillance of COVID-19 mRNA Vaccine Effectiveness and Safety: A Cohort Study Using a Large U.S. Healthcare Network
proposals.motivation: Post-authorization monitoring of COVID-19 mRNA vaccines has largely treated effectiveness and safety as separate research questions, using different study designs and data sources. This fragmented approach complicates a holistic benefit-risk assessment for patients and public health. A unified framework within a single, large-scale healthcare network is needed to concurrently evaluate real-world vaccine protection and potential adverse events, especially in populations with complex health profiles like those with psychiatric disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: Using a common data model and cohort design, we hypothesize that an integrated analysis will identify distinct temporal and demographic patterns for vaccine effectiveness against COVID-19-associated encounters and for safety signals following vaccination, and that the net benefit will remain strongly positive across key subgroups.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using the same large-scale U.S. healthcare network data (e.g., HealthVerity, TriNetX) previously employed for effectiveness studies. The primary cohort will include adults with documented mRNA vaccination. We will apply a self-controlled case series (SCCS) design to monitor safety signals (e.g., myocarditis, anaphylaxis) in pre-specified risk windows post-vaccination. Concurrently, we will use a test-negative design and Cox regression to estimate vaccine effectiveness (VE) against COVID-19 hospitalization and ED encounters during Delta and Omicron periods. Analyses will be stratified by psychiatric diagnosis, age, and vaccine dose.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a methodological blueprint for integrated post-marketing surveillance of medical countermeasures. It will deliver contemporaneous, population-stratified estimates of both VE and adverse event risks from a single data ecosystem, directly informing clinical and public health guidance. The work will also demonstrate the operational feasibility of dual monitoring within large-scale real-world data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by this team and others has assessed VE or safety separately, the novelty lies in the concurrent application of distinct, optimized study designs (SCCS for safety, test-negative/cohort for VE) to the same underlying vaccinated population and data infrastructure to produce a unified benefit-risk profile.

proposals.title: Assessing Bias in COVID-19 Vaccine Effectiveness Estimates from Undocumented Prior SARS-CoV-2 Infection: A Serological Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 observational studies of COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness (VE) often classify individuals as 'vaccinated, no prior infection' based on documented PCR/antigen tests, missing undocumented infections. This misclassification could inflate VE estimates by incorrectly assigning the immune benefit of prior infection to vaccination alone. Quantifying this bias is critical for accurate interpretation of real-world vaccine performance, especially for policy and public trust.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant proportion of individuals in the 'vaccinated, no documented prior infection' cohort possess SARS-CoV-2 anti-nucleocapsid (anti-N) antibodies, indicating prior infection. Adjusting for this serological status will meaningfully attenuate estimated vaccine effectiveness against COVID-19-associated hospitalizations and encounters.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using integrated data from a large U.S. healthcare system (2019-2023), including vaccination records, encounter diagnoses, and SARS-CoV-2 testing. We will identify a cohort of mRNA-vaccinated adults with no documented prior positive test. We will link this cohort to available serum samples from routine clinical care (e.g., biobanks, discarded lab specimens) and test for anti-N antibodies (using pre-2026 EUA assays) to establish baseline seropositivity. Using Cox proportional hazards models, we will compare VE estimates (against outcomes like hospitalization) from standard models to models adjusted for baseline seropositivity, treating it as a time-fixed covariate.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide an empirical estimate of the magnitude of bias introduced by undocumented prior infection in observational VE studies. It will offer a methodological framework for using serological data to correct such bias and deliver more accurate, adjusted VE estimates for key clinical outcomes during Delta and Omicron waves.
proposals.novelty: While prior infection is known as a confounder, this study directly quantifies its undocumented prevalence in a vaccinated cohort and its specific impact on VE point estimates using pre-existing clinical biospecimens, a practical approach not widely employed in large-scale VE research pre-2026.

proposals.title: Accounting for SARS-CoV-2 Variant Lineage in Prior Infection to Refine Estimates of Protection Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies comparing vaccine-induced and infection-induced immunity often treat prior SARS-CoV-2 infection as a uniform exposure. However, the degree of cross-protection against new variants depends heavily on the variant responsible for the initial infection. This assumption of equal prior variant exposure introduces bias, particularly in analyses spanning periods of Delta and Omicron predominance, where immune escape was pronounced.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that stratifying the 'natural immunity' group by the infecting SARS-CoV-2 variant (e.g., pre-Delta, Delta, Omicron BA.1) will reveal significant heterogeneity in protection against subsequent COVID-19-associated encounters, and that failure to account for this will misrepresent the relative effectiveness of prior infection versus vaccination.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked electronic health record and state surveillance data from a large US health system (e.g., Intermountain Healthcare) from 2020-2023. Individuals with a prior positive PCR test will be assigned a probable infecting variant using a validated algorithm based on specimen collection date and local variant prevalence data from genomic surveillance. We will use Cox proportional hazards models to estimate the hazard of COVID-19-associated emergency department or hospital encounters, comparing groups defined by vaccination status and detailed prior infection history (uninfected, vaccinated only, infected with variant X, infected with variant Y). Models will adjust for demographics, comorbidities, and time-varying community transmission.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide more precise, variant-stratified estimates of protection from prior infection, offering a nuanced view of hybrid immunity. It will deliver a methodological framework for incorporating variant data into real-world effectiveness studies, improving the accuracy of comparative effectiveness research and informing public health guidance on the value of vaccination for those with prior infections.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted differential cross-protection by variant, few real-world effectiveness studies have systematically integrated individual-level variant probabilities into the definition of the natural immunity exposure, moving beyond a simple binary 'prior infection' variable in large-scale analyses.

proposals.title: Comparative Durability of Protection Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters: mRNA Vaccination Versus Prior SARS-CoV-2 Infection Over a 12-Month Period
proposals.motivation: By late 2025, understanding the long-term durability of immunity from vaccination and prior infection remains critical for public health policy and clinical guidance. While studies like Stenehjem et al. (2022) compared protection over ~179 days, the relative waning of protection beyond six months, especially against newer Omicron subvariants, is less defined. This knowledge gap hinders optimal timing for booster recommendations and risk assessment for individuals with hybrid immunity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative effectiveness of primary mRNA vaccination versus prior SARS-CoV-2 infection will show significant differential waning over a 6- to 12-month period, with hybrid immunity (vaccination plus infection) conferring the most durable protection against severe COVID-19-associated encounters.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from a large U.S. healthcare system (e.g., Intermountain Health). Adults will be categorized into exposure cohorts: vaccinated only, prior infection only, hybrid immunity, and neither. Using a time-to-event analysis, we will estimate adjusted hazard ratios for COVID-19-associated emergency department or hospital encounters over 12 months of follow-up, starting 14 days post-vaccination or positive test. We will employ Cox proportional hazards models with time-varying covariates to account for waning, adjusting for demographics, comorbidities, prior healthcare encounters, and calendar time to control for variant predominance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first direct, longitudinal comparisons of vaccine- and infection-induced immunity over a full year in a real-world clinical setting. It will deliver actionable evidence on the timing and magnitude of waning for different immunity types, informing booster vaccine schedules and personal risk stratification. The findings will extend the methodological framework established in our prior work to a critical, longer-term horizon.
proposals.novelty: This extends our previous 179-day analysis to a full year, capturing later waning dynamics and more variant transitions. It directly compares the durability of three distinct immunity states (vaccine-only, infection-only, hybrid) over this extended period, which has not been comprehensively assessed in large-scale, real-world data.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Non-mRNA COVID-19 Vaccines (Viral Vector, Protein Subunit) Versus Prior SARS-CoV-2 Infection Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters in Adults: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: By late 2025, while mRNA vaccines have been extensively studied, real-world evidence on the durability and comparative effectiveness of other vaccine platforms (e.g., Janssen's viral vector and Novavax's protein subunit) remains less defined, especially against newer variants. Understanding the protection conferred by these alternative vaccines relative to natural infection is critical for informing public health policy, vaccine choice, and understanding population immunity, particularly for individuals with contraindications to mRNA vaccines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that protection against COVID-19-associated hospitalizations and emergency department encounters provided by completed primary series of non-mRNA vaccines is non-inferior to that provided by documented prior SARS-CoV-2 infection, but that the durability of this protection may differ by vaccine platform and variant era.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record and linked immunization data from a large U.S. healthcare system (e.g., Veterans Health Administration or Kaiser Permanente). Adults receiving a complete primary series of a non-mRNA vaccine (Janssen Ad26.COV2.S or Novavax NVX-CoV2373) will be matched 1:1 on demographic and clinical factors to adults with a documented prior PCR-positive SARS-CoV-2 infection and no vaccination. The primary outcome is a COVID-19-associated hospitalization or ED encounter. We will employ Cox proportional hazards models to estimate adjusted hazard ratios (aHRs) for vaccine vs. infection cohorts, stratified by variant era (e.g., Omicron sub-lineages) and time since exposure (e.g., 0-90, 91-180 days).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, head-to-head comparison of non-mRNA vaccine platforms against natural infection in the same analytical framework, filling a key evidence gap. It will deliver actionable data on the relative effectiveness of these vaccines, which is vital for global vaccine policy where multiple platforms are deployed and for patient-clinician decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While Stenehjem et al. have previously compared mRNA vaccination and prior infection, this analysis uniquely focuses on non-mRNA platforms, which have been less studied in direct comparison to natural immunity, especially during Omicron predominance.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of mRNA Vaccination and Prior Infection Against COVID-19 Hospitalization and Mortality in High-Risk Cohorts During Omicron Subvariant Predominance
proposals.motivation: While prior studies have quantified protection against any SARS-CoV-2 infection or encounter, the critical public health and clinical question is the prevention of severe outcomes, especially among vulnerable populations. As of late 2025, the durability and comparative effectiveness of hybrid immunity (vaccination plus prior infection) versus vaccination alone against hospitalization and death during the evolving Omicron era remains incompletely characterized, particularly for adults with comorbidities like psychiatric disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hybrid immunity (prior infection plus mRNA vaccination) provides superior and more durable protection against COVID-19-associated hospitalization and mortality compared to vaccination alone, and that this protective advantage is most pronounced in clinically vulnerable subgroups.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked electronic health record and immunization data from a large U.S. healthcare system (e.g., Veterans Health Administration or Kaiser Permanente) from December 2021 to December 2025. The primary exposure will be time-updated vaccination status (dose number and days since last dose) and documented prior SARS-CoV-2 infection. Using Cox proportional hazards models, we will estimate adjusted hazard ratios for laboratory-confirmed COVID-19 hospitalization and all-cause mortality within 30 days of diagnosis. We will employ a test-negative design as a secondary analysis to control for healthcare-seeking behavior and will stratify by age, psychiatric diagnosis, and immunocompromised status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct, comparative estimates of real-world vaccine effectiveness against the most severe clinical endpoints, informing booster policy and risk communication. It will uniquely quantify the incremental benefit of prior infection in vaccinated populations and identify subgroups with persistently lower levels of protection, guiding targeted intervention strategies.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond measuring infection or any encounter to focus explicitly on hospitalization and death, and integrates the investigator's expertise in psychiatric comorbidity and infection adjudication to examine effect modification by high-risk conditions in a contemporary Omicron context.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Hybrid Immunity Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters in Adults: A Retrospective Cohort Study of Vaccination Timing and Dose Following Documented SARS-CoV-2 Infection
proposals.motivation: By early 2025, understanding the durability and magnitude of protection from hybrid immunity (prior infection plus vaccination) remains critical for public health guidance. Prior studies have established its superiority over infection or vaccination alone, but key questions persist regarding the optimal timing of post-infection vaccination and the incremental benefit of booster doses in this population, especially against emerging Omicron subvariants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that among adults with a documented prior SARS-CoV-2 infection, receipt of a primary mRNA vaccine series will provide significant additional protection against COVID-19-associated hospitalizations and emergency department encounters, and that a first booster dose will confer further incremental protection, with the magnitude varying by time since infection and variant period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked data from a large US healthcare system (e.g., Veterans Health Administration or Kaiser Permanente) encompassing electronic health records, vaccination registries, and SARS-CoV-2 testing data from 2021-2025. The primary exposure will be vaccination status (unvaccinated post-infection, primary series, first booster) with precise timing relative to the index infection. We will use Cox proportional hazards models, adjusted for demographics, comorbidities, and healthcare encounters, to estimate hazard ratios for COVID-19-associated outcomes, stratified by variant era (Delta, Omicron BA.1/BA.2, later Omicron sublineages). We will specifically model the interaction between time since infection and vaccination dose.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide actionable evidence on the incremental benefit and optimal schedule of mRNA vaccination for individuals with prior SARS-CoV-2 infection. It will quantify the added protection of primary series and boosters against severe outcomes across variant eras, informing clinical and public health recommendations for this large subgroup of the population.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid immunity is known to be protective, this study uniquely focuses on the *timing* and *dose-specific* incremental gains within a cohort of previously infected individuals, using detailed encounter-level data to dissect variant-specific effects and durability, building directly on Stenehjem's prior work on COVID-19-associated encounters.

proposals.title: Risk Factors for Severe Breakthrough COVID-19: A Population-Based Cohort Study of Hospitalization Despite mRNA Vaccination or Prior SARS-CoV-2 Infection
proposals.motivation: While mRNA vaccines and prior infection provide strong protection against severe COVID-19, breakthrough hospitalizations still occur, representing a significant clinical and public health burden. Identifying the specific patient-level factors that confer residual risk is critical for targeting booster strategies, prophylactic therapies, and clinical monitoring. This study directly addresses the inverse of established protection research, focusing on vulnerability within ostensibly protected groups.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific demographic factors, comorbid conditions (including psychiatric disorders), and time-dependent factors (e.g., time since last vaccine dose or prior infection) are independently associated with an increased risk of COVID-19 hospitalization among vaccinated individuals and those with prior SARS-CoV-2 infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked electronic health record and vaccination data from a large US healthcare system (e.g., Veterans Health Administration or Kaiser Permanente). The cohort will include adults with ≥1 mRNA vaccine dose or documented prior infection. The primary outcome is COVID-19-associated hospitalization. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards models, adjusting for demographics and comorbidities, to identify independent risk factors. Time-dependent covariates will model waning immunity. Sensitivity analyses will stratify by variant period (Delta vs. Omicron) and prior infection/vaccination status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a clinically actionable profile of patients who remain at high risk for severe COVID-19 despite vaccination or prior infection, moving beyond aggregate measures of vaccine effectiveness. It will quantify the independent contributions of specific factors like psychiatric disorders, immunosuppression, and waning immunity to breakthrough risk. The findings will inform risk stratification models and guidelines for enhanced protection in vulnerable subgroups.
proposals.novelty: This work inverts the dominant analytical framework from estimating population-level protection to identifying individual-level susceptibility within protected cohorts. It integrates the investigator's expertise in psychiatric comorbidities and infection adjudication with novel examination of time-since-event (vaccination/infection) as a key modifiable risk factor for breakthrough.

proposals.title: Protection from mRNA Vaccination and Prior SARS-CoV-2 Infection Against Outpatient COVID-19-Associated Encounters During Omicron Subvariant Predominance
proposals.motivation: Prior work by Stenehjem et al. quantified vaccine effectiveness (VE) against severe COVID-19 outcomes like hospitalization. However, as SARS-CoV-2 evolves towards greater immune evasion (e.g., Omicron subvariants BA.4/BA.5), understanding protection against milder, outpatient illness is critical for informing public health guidance on booster timing and personal risk assessment. This gap is especially relevant given the high societal burden of mild-to-moderate COVID-19.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mRNA vaccination (primary series and booster doses) and prior SARS-CoV-2 infection provide significant, though diminished, protection against symptomatic outpatient COVID-19 encounters during Omicron subvariant circulation, with hybrid immunity (vaccination plus prior infection) conferring the highest protection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective test-negative case-control study using electronic health record data from a large U.S. integrated health system (e.g., Intermountain Healthcare). Cases are adult outpatients with a positive SARS-CoV-2 PCR test and a COVID-19-like illness encounter. Controls are outpatients with a negative test and similar clinical presentation. We will use multivariable logistic regression to estimate odds ratios, adjusting for covariates (age, comorbidities, prior infection history, calendar time). Vaccine effectiveness (VE) will be calculated as (1 - adjusted odds ratio) * 100%, stratified by vaccine dose number, time since last dose, and prior infection status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide real-world estimates of protection against symptomatic outpatient COVID-19, a key metric for understanding the population-level impact of vaccination as the pandemic enters an endemic phase. It will directly inform clinical and public health expectations for vaccine performance in preventing mild illness and potentially reducing community transmission.
proposals.novelty: While Stenehjem's prior work focused on hospitalized patients, this study applies the rigorous test-negative design to the understudied outpatient setting, providing a more complete picture of the clinical spectrum of vaccine protection during the Omicron era.

proposals.title: Protection from COVID-19 mRNA Vaccination and Prior SARS-CoV-2 Infection Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters in Pediatric Populations During Omicron Predominance
proposals.motivation: Prior work by Stenehjem et al. (2022-2023) established the protective effectiveness of mRNA vaccination and prior infection against COVID-19-associated encounters in adults. However, children and adolescents exhibit distinct immune responses, infection severity profiles, and vaccination schedules. Understanding the durability and comparative effectiveness of these immunity sources in pediatric populations is critical for informing vaccination policies and public health guidance for younger age groups.
proposals.hypothesis: Among children and adolescents, both mRNA vaccination and prior SARS-CoV-2 infection provide significant, durable protection against medically attended COVID-19 during Omicron variant predominance, but the magnitude and waning of protection differ from adult populations and by age subgroup.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked data from a large US healthcare system (e.g., Intermountain Health) and state immunization registry for individuals aged 5-17 years. The primary outcome is a COVID-19-associated encounter (ED or hospitalization). We will use a Cox proportional hazards model with time-varying covariates for vaccination status (dose and days since last dose) and documented prior infection. The model will be adjusted for demographics, comorbidities, and healthcare utilization, and stratified by age subgroups (5-11, 12-17 years) and Omicron sublineage period.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, real-world evidence quantifying and directly comparing vaccine- and infection-derived immunity against medically attended pediatric COVID-19 during Omicron. It will inform age-specific estimates of protection duration, supporting evidence-based booster recommendations and risk assessment for children.
proposals.novelty: While adult studies are established, this directly extends the investigator's prior methodological framework to pediatric cohorts, where immune dynamics are less characterized and policy decisions remain actively debated. The analysis will explicitly test for effect modification by age and pediatric vaccination history, which is novel.

proposals.title: Quantifying Bias in COVID-19 Vaccine Effectiveness Estimates from Differential Healthcare-Seeking Behavior: A Causal Modeling Approach
proposals.motivation: Observational studies of COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness (VE) rely on healthcare encounters (e.g., testing, hospitalization) as outcomes. Pre-2026 evidence suggests healthcare-seeking behavior differs between vaccinated and unvaccinated individuals, potentially due to risk perception, access, or employer requirements. If vaccinated individuals are more likely to seek testing for milder symptoms, VE estimates against less severe outcomes could be biased toward lower protection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that differential healthcare-seeking and testing behavior by vaccination status introduces significant downward bias in observational VE estimates against SARS-CoV-2 infection and mild COVID-19, but has minimal effect on VE against severe outcomes like hospitalization.
proposals.method: We will use a causal inference framework with negative control outcomes and quantitative bias analysis. Using linked EHR and vaccination data from a large US health system (2019-2023), we will estimate VE against COVID-19-associated encounters during Omicron. We will then apply a probabilistic bias-adjustment model that parameterizes the relative testing/care-seeking probability (ρ) between vaccinated and unvaccinated individuals, informed by pre-pandemic healthcare utilization patterns and negative control outcomes (e.g., encounters for non-COVID acute respiratory illnesses). Bayesian sensitivity analysis will quantify the range of plausible corrected VE estimates under varying assumptions for ρ.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework to adjust VE estimates for differential healthcare-seeking, improving the validity of observational studies. It will generate bias-corrected VE estimates for key outcomes and identify populations where this bias is most pronounced. The methods will be directly applicable to future respiratory pathogen VE studies.
proposals.novelty: While differential behavior is acknowledged as a limitation, few studies have attempted to model and quantify its magnitude and direction using pre-pandemic behavioral baselines and causal sensitivity techniques within a single, integrated analysis specific to COVID-19 VE.

proposals.title: Assessment of COVID-19-Associated Disease Severity and Healthcare Encounters by Vaccination Status and Dose: A Retrospective Cohort Study to Investigate Potential Signals of Vaccine-Enhanced Disease
proposals.motivation: While mRNA vaccines have demonstrated significant protection against severe COVID-19, rare instances of vaccine-associated enhanced respiratory disease (VAERD) have been a historical concern for other coronavirus vaccines. As of 2025, population-level studies primarily focus on vaccine effectiveness, potentially overlooking nuanced, subgroup-specific risks of increased severity. A rigorous, hypothesis-driven search for such signals is critical for comprehensive vaccine safety surveillance and public trust.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that among specific, high-risk clinical subgroups (e.g., individuals with certain psychiatric disorders or prior specific infections), receipt of a COVID-19 mRNA vaccine dose may be associated with an increased risk of severe, COVID-19-associated healthcare encounters in the immediate post-vaccination period compared to later periods or unvaccinated controls.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health records from a large US healthcare system (2019-2025). The primary outcome is a severe COVID-19-associated encounter (hospitalization, ICU admission, or ED visit meeting Sepsis-3 criteria) with confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection. We will employ a case-centered approach and a matched cohort design. Using Poisson regression and self-controlled case series (SCCS) methods, we will compare event rates in risk intervals (e.g., 0-21 days post-vaccination) versus control intervals, stratified by vaccine dose number (dose 1, 2, booster), variant period (Delta, Omicron), and key comorbidities. We will actively adjust for time-varying confounders like community incidence and prior infection status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous, pre-specified analysis to either identify and characterize any subgroup-specific signal of increased severity post-vaccination or provide robust evidence against such an association in the studied populations. It will advance methodological approaches for vaccine safety signal detection beyond standard effectiveness frameworks and inform targeted clinical monitoring and communication strategies.
proposals.novelty: This work inverts the standard vaccine effectiveness paradigm to proactively search for severity signals, applying sophisticated self-controlled methods and focusing on high-resolution clinical outcomes (Sepsis-3 adjudicated severity) rather than just infection or hospitalization. It leverages the researcher's expertise in psychiatric comorbidity and infection adjudication to investigate vulnerable subgroups not extensively studied for this specific risk.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Confounding Effect of Prior SARS-CoV-2 Infection on COVID-19 mRNA Vaccine Effectiveness Estimates in Adults During Omicron Predominance
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 observational studies of COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness (VE) often inadequately adjust for prior infection status, treating it as a binary covariate or neglecting it entirely. This is a critical methodological gap, as natural immunity provides substantial protection, and its uneven distribution between vaccinated and unvaccinated cohorts can significantly confound VE estimates. Accurately disentangling the effects of vaccination from prior infection is essential for informing public health policy and understanding true vaccine protection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that failing to properly measure and adjust for the timing and evidence level of prior SARS-CoV-2 infection leads to a systematic overestimation of mRNA vaccine effectiveness against Omicron-associated hospitalizations and emergency department encounters in adults.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health records and state immunization/registry data from a large US health system (e.g., Intermountain Healthcare). Prior infection status will be defined using a multi-category, time-varying variable incorporating PCR/antigen test results, serology (anti-N antibody) data, and diagnostic codes, adjudicated using methods from Stenehjem's prior sepsis work. We will employ extended Cox proportional hazards models with time-dependent covariates for vaccination (dose, days since) and prior infection (evidence level, time since). VE estimates from models with detailed prior infection adjustment will be compared to those from models with naive adjustment.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous, quantitative estimate of the magnitude of confounding introduced by unmeasured or poorly measured natural immunity in VE studies. It will deliver more accurate, bias-reduced estimates of COVID-19 mRNA vaccine protection against severe Omicron outcomes. The methodological framework for adjudicating and modeling prior infection will serve as a model for future observational studies of infectious disease interventions.
proposals.novelty: While the importance of prior infection is acknowledged, this study explicitly quantifies its confounding effect on VE estimates using a sophisticated, adjudicated measure of infection status, moving beyond the common use of binary or unverified historical diagnosis codes. It directly tests the assumption that prior infection is a negligible confounder in real-world VE analyses.

proposals.title: Bridging Adult Vaccine Effectiveness to Pediatric Populations: A Pharmacokinetic Analogy Applied to SARS-CoV-2 mRNA Vaccines
proposals.motivation: Estimating real-world vaccine effectiveness (VE) in pediatric and adolescent populations is critical for public health policy but methodologically challenging. Large-scale observational studies like the VISION Network have robustly estimated VE in adults, but direct replication in younger cohorts requires immense sample sizes and is resource-intensive. A structured, efficient method to leverage established adult VE frameworks for pediatric inference is urgently needed to inform timely vaccination strategies for children.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting principles from pharmacokinetic bridging studies, key pediatric-specific effect modifiers (e.g., immune maturation, variant exposure history, and dosing intervals) can be systematically identified and quantitatively adjusted for, allowing for valid bridging of VE estimates from the adult VISION Network methodology to pediatric and adolescent age groups.
proposals.method: We will apply a test-negative design within the VISION Network infrastructure to establish a reference VE model for adults (18+). For pediatric cohorts (5-17 years), we will conduct a parallel analysis, then employ a multivariable meta-regression framework to quantify the effect of modifiers like prior infection prevalence, time since last dose, and variant period. The bridging adjustment will be implemented via a Bayesian hierarchical model that treats the adult VE estimates as an informative prior, with pediatric data updating the posterior, formally accounting for between-group differences in identified modifiers.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel methodological framework for efficiently generating pediatric VE estimates by leveraging existing adult observational data. It will yield timely, adjusted estimates of mRNA vaccine effectiveness against pediatric COVID-19-associated hospitalizations and ED encounters during Omicron subvariant circulation. The approach establishes a blueprint for accelerating evidence generation for future pediatric vaccine recommendations.
proposals.novelty: This work is novel in its direct application of a clinical pharmacology regulatory concept (PK bridging) to the field of observational vaccine effectiveness research. It moves beyond simple age-stratified analyses to a formal, quantitative adjustment for developmental and epidemiological effect modifiers, a method not previously applied in VE studies pre-2026.

proposals.title: Disentangling Immunological Waning from Depletion of Susceptibles: A Multi-Cohort, Time-Varying Force-of-Infection Analysis of Long-Term mRNA Vaccine Effectiveness
proposals.motivation: Estimating the true waning of vaccine protection over time is critically important for informing booster policies. However, observed declines in vaccine effectiveness (VE) are confounded by the concurrent depletion of susceptible individuals in the vaccinated population and by time-varying community exposure to different SARS-CoV-2 variants. Pre-2026 studies, including our own, have highlighted these challenges but have not fully resolved them, leading to potential bias in long-term waning estimates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that after accounting for the time-varying force of infection and the depletion of susceptibles within vaccinated cohorts, the intrinsic waning of mRNA vaccine-conferred immunity against severe COVID-19 is slower than previously reported in observational studies.
proposals.method: We will implement a multi-cohort, longitudinal study using a test-negative design within a large US healthcare system. Individuals will be tracked from their primary mRNA vaccination series. We will employ continuous-time survival models (e.g., extended Cox models) where the hazard function explicitly incorporates a time-varying, variant-specific community force of infection, estimated from local surveillance data. The models will adjust for time-updated prior infection status (confirmed by PCR/antigen test) and key covariates. This approach directly models the shrinking pool of susceptibles to isolate the decay of immunological protection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, bias-reduced estimate of the intrinsic waning curve of mRNA vaccines against COVID-19 hospitalization. It will deliver a rigorous analytical framework for separating immunological waning from epidemiological confounding, a method applicable to future vaccine effectiveness studies for respiratory pathogens.
proposals.novelty: While test-negative designs are standard, the explicit, dynamic modeling of the force of infection and the susceptible pool as time-varying parameters in survival analysis to directly address the 'depletion of susceptibles' bias represents a significant methodological advance beyond current pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: A Damage Accumulation Model for Waning Vaccine Immunity: Applying Material Fatigue Theory to SARS-CoV-2 Variant Exposures
proposals.motivation: Understanding the waning of COVID-19 vaccine protection over time and across SARS-CoV-2 variants remains a critical public health challenge. Current models often treat waning as a simple function of time-since-vaccination, failing to mechanistically account for the differential immune stress posed by antigenically distinct variants like Delta and Omicron. A more dynamic model is needed to forecast protection in the face of unpredictable viral evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: The decline in vaccine effectiveness against SARS-CoV-2 infection can be modeled as a cumulative damage process, where each period of exposure to a circulating variant inflicts a quantifiable 'immune stress' proportional to its antigenic distance from the vaccine strain, leading to a predictable loss of protection.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Palmgren-Miner linear damage accumulation rule from material fatigue. Using longitudinal data on COVID-19-associated encounters and vaccination status from large healthcare systems (e.g., VISION Network), we will define variant-specific stress cycles based on variant prevalence and estimated antigenic distance. We will fit an S-N curve analog, where 'S' is the antigenic stress magnitude and 'N' is the number of exposure days before a failure event (breakthrough infection). Model parameters will be estimated via survival analysis, with time-varying variant exposure as the key covariate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanism-informed framework for quantifying waning immunity that integrates virologic (variant evolution) and immunologic (waning) data. It will yield a predictive tool to estimate remaining vaccine protection under specific variant landscapes, informing the timing of booster doses. The approach will move the field beyond empirical time-based decay models.
proposals.novelty: This is the first application of material fatigue theory, a well-established engineering paradigm, to model immunological waning. It explicitly models variant sequence as cyclic stress, a non-obvious cross-disciplinary analogy that provides a formal structure for cumulative antigenic pressure.

proposals.title: Establishing a Global Vaccine Effectiveness (VE) Network: A Multi-Country Test-Negative Design Study to Compare COVID-19 mRNA Vaccine Effectiveness Against Emerging SARS-CoV-2 Variants
proposals.motivation: As SARS-CoV-2 continues to evolve, understanding how vaccine effectiveness (VE) varies by variant and across different populations and healthcare systems is critical for global public health policy. The US-based VISION Network has proven the utility of large-scale, real-world data using the test-negative design, but its findings may not generalize globally. Establishing a harmonized, international network is necessary to rapidly detect signals of immune escape and inform vaccine deployment strategies worldwide.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying a standardized VISION Network methodology across diverse countries, we will identify significant heterogeneity in mRNA vaccine effectiveness against severe COVID-19 outcomes during periods of Omicron subvariant predominance, and that this heterogeneity will be partially explained by differences in prior infection exposure, vaccine dosing schedules, and circulating sublineages.
proposals.method: We will replicate the core VISION Network test-negative design across partner sites in 6-8 countries on multiple continents. The method involves identifying patients with COVID-19-like illness who are tested for SARS-CoV-2 in emergency or inpatient settings. Using logistic regression, we will estimate VE against COVID-19-associated hospitalizations, comparing odds of vaccination (primary series and boosters, with dose-specific intervals) among test-positive cases versus test-negative controls. The analysis will be stratified by dominant variant period (e.g., BA.1, BA.2, BA.4/BA.5) determined by local genomic surveillance. Key covariates will include age, comorbidities, prior documented infection, and calendar time.
proposals.contribution: This work will create the first operational framework for a global, rapid-response VE monitoring network using a harmonized protocol. It will provide the most comprehensive, comparative estimates of VE across major global regions during a critical phase of the pandemic, offering actionable evidence for national and international health agencies. The network infrastructure will be poised to assess future variant-specific VE and other medical countermeasures.
proposals.novelty: While individual countries conduct VE studies, no pre-2026 effort has successfully implemented a *standardized*, high-throughput, test-negative design protocol simultaneously across multiple, diverse healthcare systems to produce directly comparable, variant-stratified VE estimates in near real-time.

proposals.title: Effectiveness of mRNA Booster Doses Against Progression to Critical Illness and Mortality in Adults During Omicron Variant Predominance: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: By late 2025, mRNA booster vaccination has demonstrated robust protection against COVID-19 hospitalization, especially in high-risk groups like those with psychiatric disorders. However, real-world evidence on booster effectiveness (VE) against the most severe clinical endpoints—ICU admission, mechanical ventilation, and death—during Omicron predominance remains less characterized. Quantifying VE against these critical outcomes is essential for informing public health policy and clinical risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mRNA booster doses provide significant, though potentially attenuated compared to earlier variants, protection against progression to critical illness and death following SARS-CoV-2 infection during periods of Omicron variant circulation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked data from a large U.S. healthcare system (e.g., VA, Kaiser Permanente) covering the Omicron-predominant period (e.g., Dec 2021 - Nov 2022). The cohort will include adults with a documented SARS-CoV-2 positive test. Exposure will be booster vaccination status (≥1 booster dose vs. primary series only), with time-updated Cox proportional hazards models adjusting for demographics, comorbidities, prior infection, and calendar time. The primary outcome is a composite of ICU admission, mechanical ventilation, or COVID-19-associated death within 30 days of positive test. We will calculate adjusted hazard ratios and VE as (1 – aHR)*100%.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide precise, real-world estimates of booster VE against the most severe spectrum of COVID-19 outcomes in the Omicron era, directly addressing a key evidence gap. It will inform clinicians and policymakers about the value of boosters in preventing critical resource utilization and mortality, complementing existing hospitalization data.
proposals.novelty: While Stenehjem et al. have assessed VE against 'COVID-19-associated encounters,' this proposal explicitly focuses on adjudicated, hierarchically severe endpoints beyond hospitalization admission, leveraging methods from sepsis diagnosis adjudication to ensure outcome specificity in a post-vaccination, Omicron-context cohort.

proposals.title: Individual-Level Immunologic Correlates of Protection Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters Following a Third mRNA Vaccine Dose: A Nested Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, population-level vaccine effectiveness (VE) studies, including those by Stenehjem et al., have robustly quantified protection against COVID-19-associated encounters. However, these network estimates mask substantial individual heterogeneity in immune response. Defining the specific antibody titer thresholds that correlate with protection at the individual level is critical for personalizing booster strategies, evaluating waning immunity, and informing future vaccine development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that post-third-dose anti-spike IgG antibody titers are a strong, modifiable correlate of protection, and that specific quantitative thresholds can be identified that predict a significantly reduced risk of symptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection during periods of Omicron variant predominance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a nested case-control study within a large, pre-existing healthcare system cohort of adults receiving a third mRNA vaccine dose. Cases (individuals with a breakthrough COVID-19-associated encounter) will be matched 1:3 to controls by age, comorbidity burden, and vaccination date. Archived serum samples, collected 2-8 weeks post-third dose as part of routine care or prior studies, will be assayed for anti-spike IgG using a standardized quantitative assay (e.g., Roche Elecsys). We will use conditional logistic regression to model the odds of breakthrough infection as a function of log-transformed antibody titer, adjusting for prior infection history and time since vaccination, and will estimate the protective titer threshold using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will bridge the gap between population-level VE and individual-level immunology by providing quantitative, clinically actionable antibody thresholds associated with protection against contemporary variants. It will directly inform clinical guidance on the utility of post-vaccination serologic testing and provide a model for evaluating correlates of protection for future vaccine regimens.
proposals.novelty: While prior work established population-level VE of third doses, this study uniquely links those real-world effectiveness estimates to measured individual immunologic data within the same healthcare network, moving beyond ecological correlation to establish a direct, quantifiable biological link.

proposals.title: Modeling the Impact of COVID-19 Booster Campaigns on Emergency Department and Hospital Bed Occupancy: A Retrospective Cohort and Simulation Study
proposals.motivation: During the Omicron and subsequent variant waves, healthcare systems faced severe strain from high volumes of COVID-19-associated encounters, threatening care quality for all patients. Understanding the direct effect of vaccination on acute care utilization, beyond individual protection, is critical for operational planning and public health policy. This study addresses the dual problem of how booster campaigns influence system-level capacity metrics, a key gap in pre-2026 health services research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher population-level booster dose coverage is associated with significant, measurable reductions in the rates of COVID-19-associated emergency department visits and hospital bed-days occupied, thereby preserving system capacity during periods of high viral transmission.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked electronic health record and immunization data from a large US healthcare system (e.g., Intermountain Health, 2021-2023). Using time-series regression (ARIMA) and Poisson models, we will quantify the association between weekly booster coverage among adults and age-stratified rates of COVID-19-associated ED/hospital encounters. We will then develop a discrete-event simulation model, parameterized with these estimates, to project bed occupancy under varying booster campaign timing and uptake scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for predicting how vaccination drives affect acute care capacity, moving beyond individual efficacy to population-level operational impact. It will offer actionable evidence for hospital administrators and public health officials to optimize the timing and targeting of booster campaigns to mitigate system strain during future surges.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Stenehjem et al. quantified individual-level vaccine protection against encounters, this study uniquely integrates those estimates into a dynamic system-level model of bed occupancy. It directly links vaccination epidemiology to real-time hospital operations, a novel synthesis of clinical and operational research perspectives.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Validity of Non-COVID Respiratory Illness Controls in Test-Negative Design Studies of COVID-19 Vaccine Effectiveness
proposals.motivation: The test-negative design (TND) is a cornerstone of real-world COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness (VE) estimation, relying on non-COVID acute respiratory illness (ARI) patients as controls to approximate the vaccination behavior of the source population. However, if healthcare-seeking behavior or underlying risk for ARI is correlated with vaccination status, this control group may misrepresent the exposure odds in true SARS-CoV-2 susceptibles, introducing bias. Validating this core TND assumption is critical for interpreting the vast body of pre-2026 VE literature, especially for subgroups like those with psychiatric disorders where care patterns may differ.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals seeking care for non-COVID ARIs have systematically different COVID-19 vaccination histories and booster uptake timing compared to a cohort of true population-based SARS-CoV-2 susceptibles, leading to measurable bias in VE estimates derived from the standard TND.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked EHR and immunization data from a large US healthcare system (e.g., Intermountain Health, Kaiser Permanente). We will identify a 'true susceptible' cohort: individuals without prior SARS-CoV-2 infection (by PCR/antigen test) at the start of an Omicron subvariant period. We will then emulate a TND within this cohort, comparing vaccination odds between lab-confirmed COVID-19 cases and two control groups: 1) the standard TND control (non-COVID ARI, test-negative), and 2) a randomly sampled control from the susceptible cohort not seeking ARI care. We will use multivariable logistic regression to estimate odds ratios and compute derived VE, comparing estimates from the two control definitions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence on the magnitude and direction of potential selection bias in TND studies of COVID-19 VE. It will offer methodological guidance for future VE studies and inform sensitivity analyses for past studies, particularly in vulnerable populations. The findings will strengthen the evidence base for vaccine policy by clarifying the validity of a widely used study design.
proposals.novelty: While the TND is widely used, direct empirical validation of its core control group assumption against a benchmark of known susceptibles in the COVID-19 era is lacking. This study inverts the typical TND application to test its foundational premise rather than simply reporting VE.

proposals.title: Comparative Vaccine Effectiveness of a Third Primary Dose vs. a First Booster Dose Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters in Adults During Omicron Predominance
proposals.motivation: By late 2025, mRNA COVID-19 vaccine regimens for adults commonly include three doses, but this group combines two distinct populations with potentially different immune responses: immunocompromised individuals receiving a third primary dose and the general population receiving a first booster. Current vaccine effectiveness (VE) studies often group them together, which may obscure important differences in protection and hinder precise public health guidance for these vulnerable groups.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that vaccine effectiveness (VE) against COVID-19-associated emergency department and hospital encounters differs significantly between adults receiving a third primary mRNA dose due to immunocompromising conditions and adults receiving a first booster dose, during periods of Omicron variant predominance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a test-negative case-control study using electronic health records from a large US healthcare system (e.g., Intermountain Healthcare or VA) from December 2021 to December 2025. Cases are adults with a positive SARS-CoV-2 test during an ED/hospital encounter; controls test negative. Exposure will be meticulously classified using vaccination dates and clinical codes into: 1) three-dose primary series (third dose 28+ days after second, with immunocompromise diagnosis), 2) two-dose primary + first booster (third dose ≥5 months after second, no immunocompromise). We will calculate adjusted odds ratios using multivariable logistic regression, controlling for age, comorbidities, prior infection, and calendar time, to estimate VE (1-OR) for each group separately.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, real-world comparison of VE for these distinct three-dose regimens, offering evidence to refine clinical and public health recommendations. It will clarify whether immunocompromised individuals, despite an additional primary dose, experience lower protection than boosted non-immunocompromised adults, informing potential needs for additional prophylactic measures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Stenehjem et al. and others has evaluated VE for 3-dose recipients, no study has explicitly disaggregated and compared the immunocompromised third-primary-dose group from the general population booster group, a critical distinction masked in aggregated '3-dose' analyses.

proposals.title: Time-to-Event Analysis of mRNA Vaccine Waning Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters in High-Risk Populations During Omicron Subvariant Circulation
proposals.motivation: Understanding the precise waning of vaccine protection over time is critical for informing booster timing, especially for high-risk groups like those with psychiatric disorders. Prior studies using odds ratios over broad time intervals provide limited granularity on the continuous risk trajectory. A survival analysis framework can quantify the instantaneous risk of breakthrough infection, offering a more dynamic model of waning immunity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hazard of a COVID-19-associated healthcare encounter increases non-linearly with time since the last mRNA vaccine dose, and that this waning pattern is significantly accelerated in populations with psychiatric disorders compared to the general adult population.
proposals.method: We will apply Cox proportional hazards models to a retrospective cohort from a large US healthcare system, defining time zero as the date of full vaccination or most recent booster. The primary endpoint is time to first COVID-19-associated emergency department or hospital encounter, with censoring at death, disenrollment, or study end. We will model time-varying vaccine effectiveness using restricted cubic splines for time since vaccination and stratify analyses by psychiatric diagnosis status, adjusting for demographics, comorbidities, prior infection, and calendar time to account for variant predominance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a detailed, time-resolved estimate of mRNA vaccine waning, identifying the point where the hazard of breakthrough begins to accelerate significantly. It will directly inform evidence-based booster schedules for vulnerable populations and demonstrate the utility of survival analysis for vaccine effectiveness research beyond simple logistic regression.
proposals.novelty: While survival methods are standard in oncology, their application to model continuous waning of COVID-19 vaccine protection in real-world, high-risk cohorts is novel. Integrating time-varying effect modeling via splines within the Cox framework to capture non-linear waning represents a methodological advance over prior interval-based VE studies.

proposals.title: A Utility-Optimized, Dynamic Model for COVID-19 Booster Scheduling: Integrating Waning Immunity and Variant-Specific Risk
proposals.motivation: By late 2025, COVID-19 booster policy remains reactive, relying on fixed intervals or broad age-based thresholds, despite clear evidence of waning vaccine effectiveness over time and differential risk by variant (e.g., Delta, Omicron). This one-size-fits-all approach fails to efficiently allocate limited public health resources or account for the heterogeneous net benefit of vaccination across individuals and over time. A formal framework is needed to quantify the trade-offs between protection benefits and potential harms (e.g., side effects, program costs) to guide personalized, adaptive scheduling.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic scheduling algorithm that maximizes expected utility—by integrating individual-level risk, time-varying vaccine effectiveness, variant prevalence, and outcome-specific utilities—will identify booster schedules that provide greater net population benefit compared to static, calendar-based recommendations.
proposals.method: We will develop a discrete-time, stochastic decision model. Inputs will include pre-2026 longitudinal data on vaccine effectiveness (VE) against infection and severe disease by variant and time-since-vaccination (modeled using parametric survival or Poisson regression, akin to Stenehjem's prior work). We will assign utilities (e.g., QALY weights) to health states (no infection, breakthrough, hospitalization) and disutilities to vaccination side effects. The core algorithm will be a finite-horizon, value iteration-based optimization that, for a given individual profile and real-time variant mix, computes the optimal vaccination time to maximize cumulative expected utility over a defined planning horizon.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept, quantitative decision-support tool for public health officials to move from static to adaptive booster policies. It will formally demonstrate how utility theory can be operationalized in vaccination policy, offering a framework for resource-efficient, personalized scheduling that can be updated with new VE evidence. The model outputs will illustrate trade-offs and optimal timing under different scenarios of waning and variant emergence.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized waning VE, this integrates those estimates into a formal utility-maximizing optimization framework for scheduling, moving beyond descriptive risk assessment to prescriptive, dynamic policy recommendation. It explicitly models the competing 'utilities' of protection and harm over time, which is novel in infectious disease epidemiology.

proposals.title: Comparative Waning of mRNA Vaccine Effectiveness Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters in Children (5-11) Versus Adolescents (12-17): A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Prior studies of COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness (VE) waning often pool pediatric age groups or focus on adults, potentially masking critical differences in immune response dynamics between younger children and adolescents. Understanding age-stratified waning is crucial for optimizing pediatric booster timing and public health policy, especially as Omicron subvariants continue to circulate. This addresses a key evidence gap in tailoring vaccination strategies for pediatric populations.
proposals.hypothesis: The rate of waning of mRNA vaccine effectiveness against documented SARS-CoV-2 infection (COVID-19-associated encounters) is significantly slower in children aged 5-11 years compared to adolescents aged 12-17 years following the primary series.
