proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record and immunization data from a large US healthcare system (e.g., Intermountain Health). The study period will cover Delta and Omicron predominance (2021-2023). Using a test-negative design, we will compare vaccination status among cases (positive PCR test) and controls (negative test). VE will be calculated by time since vaccination (e.g., 0-30, 31-90, 91-150, 151+ days) using multivariable logistic regression adjusted for confounders (demographics, comorbidities, prior infection, calendar time). The primary analysis will formally test for interaction between age subgroup (5-11 vs. 12-17) and time-since-vaccination on the odds of infection.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, comparative estimates of VE waning kinetics between these two pediatric age groups, informing the debate on differential immune maturation and vaccine response. The findings will offer evidence-based guidance for public health officials on whether age-specific booster recommendations are warranted for children and adolescents.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described waning in broad age groups, a formal, head-to-head comparison of waning rates between these specific pediatric cohorts, using robust methods to control for confounding by time and variant, has not been comprehensively performed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Risk Factors and Protective Behaviors Associated with Non-COVID-19 Acute Respiratory Illness Encounters During the Omicron Era: A Test-Negative Case-Control Inversion
proposals.motivation: The test-negative design (TND) is a cornerstone of COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness (VE) studies, using test-negative patients as controls. However, the factors driving healthcare-seeking for COVID-like illness (CLI) in those who test negative are poorly understood. Characterizing this 'control' group is crucial for interpreting VE estimates and understanding the broader burden of non-COVID respiratory infections on healthcare systems during the pandemic.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific demographic factors, health behaviors (e.g., mask use, social distancing), and prior SARS-CoV-2 infection or vaccination status are independently associated with seeking care for CLI while testing negative for SARS-CoV-2 during periods of Omicron variant predominance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from a large US health system (e.g., Intermountain Healthcare). Adults with an emergency department or urgent care encounter for CLI and a resultant SARS-CoV-2 PCR test from December 2021 to June 2022 will be included. We will 'invert' the TND, defining cases as test-negative encounters and controls as test-positive encounters. Using multivariable logistic regression, we will model the odds of a test-negative encounter, adjusting for age, sex, comorbidities, number of prior vaccine doses, prior documented infection, and calendar time. We will specifically evaluate the association of prior infection/vaccination, using time-since-last-event as a key variable.
proposals.contribution: This study will quantify the patient-level drivers of non-COVID CLI healthcare use during a high-transmission period, providing context for the control groups used in VE studies. It will elucidate whether prior immune history protects against CLI broadly or specifically against SARS-CoV-2. Findings can inform public health messaging and resource planning for respiratory illness seasons.
proposals.novelty: While TND is widely used for VE, systematically analyzing the test-negative control group as the primary outcome of interest is a novel inversion of the standard paradigm. It leverages the established TND infrastructure to answer a distinct but complementary public health question.

proposals.title: Effectiveness of a Third mRNA COVID-19 Vaccine Dose Against Omicron-Associated Encounters in Adolescents Aged 12-15 Years: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: By late 2025, mRNA booster doses for adolescents are widely recommended, but real-world effectiveness data for the 12-15 year age group during Omicron subvariant predominance remain sparse. This gap is critical as adolescents are key vectors of community transmission and experience significant educational and social disruption from infection. Quantifying the incremental protection of a booster in this population informs public health policy and parental decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that receipt of a third (booster) mRNA COVID-19 vaccine dose provides significant additional protection against documented SARS-CoV-2 infection and COVID-19-associated healthcare encounters among adolescents aged 12-15 years, compared to those who received only a primary series, during periods of Omicron variant predominance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from a large US healthcare system. The cohort will include adolescents aged 12-15 who completed a primary mRNA vaccine series. We will define exposure as receipt of a booster dose. The primary outcome is a COVID-19-associated encounter (positive test, outpatient, ED, or hospitalization). We will use a Cox proportional hazards model with time-varying vaccination status, adjusting for demographics, comorbidities, prior infection, and calendar time. Vaccine effectiveness will be calculated as (1 – hazard ratio) * 100%. We will employ a test-negative design as a secondary analysis to control for healthcare-seeking behavior.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first comprehensive estimates of booster dose effectiveness specifically in the 12-15 year adolescent population against the Omicron variant. It will directly inform CDC ACIP recommendations on booster timing and necessity for this age group. The findings will also contribute to understanding waning immunity post-primary series in adolescents.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Stenehjem's prior work evaluated protection in adults and included prior infection, this proposal focuses exclusively on the understudied adolescent cohort and isolates the incremental benefit of a third dose beyond the primary series. It leverages established methods from her recent papers but applies them to a new, policy-relevant demographic question.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of mRNA COVID-19 Vaccination Against Severe Outcomes in Immunocompromised Adults During Omicron Predominance
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies of COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness (VE) often exclude immunocompromised (IC) individuals or analyze them as a heterogeneous subgroup, obscuring crucial differences in protection. This population faces higher risks of severe COVID-19, and real-world VE data against Omicron variants in specific IC conditions (e.g., solid organ transplant, hematologic malignancies, autoimmune disease) remain limited. Understanding the durability and comparative effectiveness of primary series and booster doses in these groups is essential for informing clinical guidance and vaccination policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mRNA vaccine effectiveness against COVID-19-associated hospitalization and emergency department encounters will be significantly lower and wane more rapidly in immunocompromised adults compared to an immunocompetent control group during Omicron predominance, with substantial variation across specific immunocompromising conditions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked electronic health record and immunization data from a large U.S. healthcare system (e.g., Veterans Health Administration or Kaiser Permanente). The cohort will include adults with specific immunocompromising conditions (defined by diagnosis codes and medication fills) and a matched immunocompetent control. The primary outcome is a COVID-19-associated hospital encounter. We will use a Cox proportional hazards model with time-varying vaccination status (unvaccinated, primary series, boosted) to estimate hazard ratios, adjusting for demographics, comorbidities, prior infection, and healthcare utilization. VE will be calculated as (1 – hazard ratio) × 100%. We will stratify analyses by specific IC condition and time since last vaccine dose.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide condition-specific, real-world estimates of mRNA VE against severe Omicron outcomes in key immunocompromised populations, directly addressing a critical evidence gap. It will quantify the differential waning of protection compared to the general population and inform the clinical need for and timing of additional booster doses or prophylactic therapies in these high-risk groups.
proposals.novelty: This work inverts the common 'immunocompetent only' analytical filter by making immunocompromised populations the exclusive focus, moving beyond broad subgroup analyses to deliver granular, condition-specific VE estimates that are urgently needed for personalized clinical decision-making.

proposals.title: Comparative Vaccine Effectiveness of Monovalent mRNA Primary Series Products Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters in Adults During Omicron Predominance
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, real-world vaccine effectiveness (VE) studies for COVID-19 have often aggregated all monovalent mRNA doses, treating the BNT162b2 (Pfizer-BioNTech) and mRNA-1273 (Moderna) primary series as equivalent. However, pre-clinical and early clinical data suggest differences in immunogenicity and durability. As public health moves towards refined booster strategies, understanding whether initial product choice confers differential long-term protection, especially against severe outcomes during variant eras, is critical for evidence-based policy and clinical guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the specific monovalent mRNA vaccine product (BNT162b2 vs. mRNA-1273) received for the primary series is associated with differential risks of COVID-19-associated emergency department encounters and hospitalizations during periods of Omicron variant predominance, after adjusting for time since vaccination and key confounders.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective test-negative case-control study using electronic health record and immunization data from a large US healthcare system (e.g., Intermountain Healthcare). Cases will be adults with COVID-19-like illness and a positive SARS-CoV-2 test; controls will be those with a negative test. The exposure will be the specific product (BNT162b2 or mRNA-1273) of the completed primary series. We will use multivariable logistic regression to estimate adjusted odds ratios, comparing VE for each product against the other and against unvaccinated individuals, stratified by outcome severity (ED encounter vs. hospitalization) and time since vaccination (e.g., 14-90 days, 91-180 days). Analyses will control for age, comorbidities, prior infection, and calendar time.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, direct comparison of real-world effectiveness between the two main mRNA primary series regimens through the Omicron era. It will inform whether initial product selection has lasting implications for protection against healthcare encounters. The findings could guide more personalized vaccination recommendations and refine models for predicting population-level immunity.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Stenehjem et al. and others has examined VE by dose number and prior infection, no major study has disaggregated the primary series by specific product to test the assumption of equivalence in real-world effectiveness against Omicron-associated outcomes.

proposals.title: Comparative Vaccine Effectiveness of Bivalent mRNA Boosters Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters Among Immunocompromised Adults During Omicron BA.4/BA.5 and BQ.1/BQ.1.1 Predominance
proposals.motivation: Immunocompromised individuals have been consistently shown to have diminished immune responses to COVID-19 vaccination and are at high risk for severe outcomes. Prior vaccine effectiveness (VE) studies, including those by this research group, have often excluded or underpowered analyses for this heterogeneous population. As of late 2025, real-world evidence on the protection conferred by the bivalent (Original/BA.4-5) mRNA boosters specifically in immunocompromised adults against the evolving Omicron sublineages remains limited and critically needed to inform clinical and public health guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bivalent mRNA booster vaccination provides significant but attenuated protection against COVID-19-associated emergency department and urgent care encounters and hospitalizations among immunocompromised adults compared to immunocompetent adults, with effectiveness varying by immunocompromising condition and time since vaccination.
proposals.method: We will conduct a test-negative case-control study using integrated electronic health record and immunization data from a large U.S. healthcare system (e.g., Kaiser Permanente, Intermountain Health). Immunocompromised status will be defined using diagnosis and medication codes for solid organ transplant, stem cell transplant, HIV, cancer, and autoimmune conditions. Cases (positive SARS-CoV-2 test) and controls (negative test) will be matched on encounter date, site, and immunocompromised category. VE will be calculated as (1 – adjusted odds ratio) × 100% using multivariable logistic regression, adjusting for age, sex, comorbidities, prior infection, and prior vaccine dose count and timing. Analyses will be stratified by immunocompromised subgroup and clinical outcome severity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first detailed, real-world assessments of bivalent booster VE in a broad immunocompromised population during later Omicron subvariant circulation. It will quantify the level of protection against moderate and severe COVID-19 and identify which patient subgroups derive the most and least benefit, directly informing risk assessment and booster prioritization for this vulnerable group.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has established lower VE in immunocompromised people for monovalent vaccines, this study focuses specifically on the bivalent booster's performance against contemporaneous Omicron subvariants (BA.4/BA.5, BQ.1) and provides granular, condition-specific VE estimates not widely available in the literature as of 2025.

proposals.title: Durability of Bivalent mRNA Vaccine Effectiveness Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters Over a 12-Month Period in a Large US Health System
proposals.motivation: Following the widespread deployment of bivalent (Original/Omicron BA.4/BA.5) mRNA COVID-19 boosters in late 2022, understanding the long-term durability of protection is critical for public health policy and potential revaccination schedules. Prior studies on monovalent vaccine waning were limited to shorter follow-up periods or earlier variants, leaving a key evidence gap for the durability of bivalent booster protection against Omicron sublineages over extended time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that protection against COVID-19-associated emergency department and hospital encounters conferred by a bivalent mRNA booster dose will wane significantly over a 6-12 month period post-vaccination, with the rate of waning differing by age group and immunocompromised status.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record and immunization data from a large US health system (e.g., Intermountain Healthcare). Adults receiving a bivalent booster will be followed from day 14 post-vaccination for up to 12 months. The primary outcome is a COVID-19-associated encounter (ED visit or hospitalization). We will use a Cox proportional hazards model with time-updated vaccination status to estimate adjusted hazard ratios, comparing person-time in defined post-booster intervals (e.g., 14-90 days, 91-180 days, 181-365 days) to unvaccinated or vaccinated-but-unboosted person-time, adjusting for demographics, comorbidities, prior infection history, and calendar time.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first comprehensive, long-term estimates of bivalent booster durability against clinically significant outcomes, directly informing CDC ACIP guidance on booster timing. It will quantify the rate of waning in key clinical subgroups, helping to tailor recommendations for vulnerable populations.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Stenehjem's prior work analyzed waning during Delta and early Omicron for monovalent vaccines, this proposal extends the methodological framework to assess the bivalent booster over a full 12-month period, a duration not yet fully characterized in the literature as of 2025.

proposals.title: Risk Factors for Bivalent mRNA Vaccine Failure: A Case-Control Study of Breakthrough COVID-19 Encounters in a Large US Health System, 2022-2024
proposals.motivation: While bivalent mRNA boosters significantly reduce severe COVID-19, breakthrough infections requiring emergency or urgent care (ED/UC) visits persist, straining healthcare systems. Identifying the patient-level factors associated with this clinical vaccine failure is critical for refining public health strategies, targeting additional protective measures, and informing patient risk counseling. Prior work has focused on vaccine effectiveness in broad populations, but a dedicated analysis of factors for failure among the boosted is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that breakthrough ED/UC visits following bivalent boosting are associated with specific demographic factors (e.g., older age), comorbid conditions (e.g., immunocompromising diagnoses), and time-dependent factors (e.g., time since last vaccine dose or prior infection).
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study within the Providence St. Joseph Health system. Cases will be adults with a bivalent mRNA booster (≥14 days prior) who subsequently have an ED/UC encounter with a confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection (PCR/antigen) during Omicron subvariant predominance (late 2022-2024). Controls will be boosted adults without a documented COVID-19 encounter, matched on health system region and index date. Using multivariable conditional logistic regression, we will model the association between breakthrough status and candidate risk factors extracted from EHRs: demographics, comorbidities (using ICD codes), prior infection history, time since last vaccine dose, and concomitant medication use (e.g., immunosuppressants).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a granular profile of patients at highest risk for clinically significant vaccine failure despite current boosting recommendations. It will directly inform clinical practice by identifying subgroups that may benefit from enhanced surveillance, early antiviral therapy, or additional booster doses. The findings will add a critical 'vaccine failure' perspective to the literature, complementing existing vaccine effectiveness studies.
proposals.novelty: This work inverts the standard vaccine effectiveness paradigm to focus specifically on failure among the bivalent-boosted, a rapidly growing population in the Omicron era. It leverages detailed, longitudinal EHR data to adjudicate outcomes and exposures with high clinical fidelity, moving beyond claims-based analyses.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness and Waning of Fourth and Additional mRNA Booster Doses Against COVID-19-Associated Encounters in High-Risk Adult Populations
proposals.motivation: By late 2025, mRNA vaccine booster strategies have evolved to include fourth and additional doses, particularly for older adults and those with immunocompromising conditions. However, real-world evidence on the magnitude, durability, and comparative effectiveness of these additional booster doses, especially against Omicron subvariants, remains limited. Quantifying this protection is critical for informing public health recommendations on booster frequency and identifying populations with the greatest need.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that receipt of a fourth or additional mRNA booster dose provides significant, time-dependent protection against COVID-19-associated emergency department and hospital encounters, with the magnitude and waning of protection differing meaningfully from primary series or first booster protection and varying by clinical risk group.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record and immunization data from a large U.S. healthcare system (e.g., Intermountain Health, 2015-2025). The primary exposure will be time-varying vaccination status, categorized by dose number and days since the most recent dose. The primary outcome is a COVID-19-associated encounter, defined using a validated adjudication algorithm from prior work. We will employ extended Cox proportional hazards models with time-dependent covariates to estimate adjusted hazard ratios for infection and severe disease, stratified by age, immunocompromised status, and psychiatric comorbidity. Key confounders will include prior SARS-CoV-2 infection (documented via PCR/antigen testing), demographics, and clinical comorbidities.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first comprehensive, real-world analyses of protection conferred by fourth and subsequent mRNA booster doses in a diverse adult population. It will directly inform clinical and public health guidance on optimal booster timing for high-risk groups. The findings will extend the evidence base on vaccine effectiveness waning into the multi-booster era.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies by this team and others have characterized waning after primary series and first boosters, this analysis focuses specifically on the understudied additional booster doses. It integrates the investigator's expertise in psychiatric comorbidity and rigorous infection adjudication to examine effect modification in key vulnerable subgroups.

proposals.title: Modeling the Optimal Timing of COVID-19 mRNA Booster Doses to Minimize Severe Outcomes in High-Risk Populations
proposals.motivation: By late 2025, COVID-19 mRNA vaccines demonstrate waning protection against severe outcomes, necessitating booster doses. However, the ideal interval between doses to balance population-level protection, logistical feasibility, and resource constraints remains unclear, especially for high-risk groups like those with psychiatric disorders. Determining an evidence-based, optimal schedule is critical for public health policy to prevent hospitalizations and deaths.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a simulation model incorporating detailed waning curves and population risk stratification will identify a booster interval that minimizes total severe COVID-19 encounters (hospitalizations, ED visits) more effectively than fixed, calendar-based recommendations.
proposals.method: We will develop a discrete-time simulation model using a large, pre-2026 US healthcare database (e.g., VISION Network, similar to Stenehjem's prior work). Inputs will include empirical estimates of vaccine effectiveness (VE) waning against severe outcomes (Delta, Omicron) and prior infection protection. The model will simulate individuals with varying risk profiles (e.g., psychiatric diagnosis, age) and track vaccination status, infection history, and severe outcomes over time. We will use a grid-search optimization algorithm to evaluate booster intervals (e.g., 4-12 months) and identify the interval minimizing total predicted severe encounters over a 2-year period.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a quantitative framework for determining booster timing based on dynamic waning data and heterogeneous risk, moving beyond one-size-fits-all schedules. It will directly inform public health guidance by identifying intervals that maximize protection for vulnerable populations and optimize healthcare resource use.
proposals.novelty: While prior work characterized waning VE, this study uniquely integrates these curves into a simulation model to *optimize* timing for future doses, explicitly accounting for competing risks of prior infection and differential risk in subgroups like psychiatric patients, a population of established interest to the PI.

proposals.title: Active Surveillance for Rare Cardiovascular and Neurological Events Following COVID-19 mRNA Vaccination Using a Large-Scale Health System Cohort
proposals.motivation: While mRNA COVID-19 vaccines have demonstrated clear efficacy, public confidence requires rigorous, transparent safety monitoring, especially for rare but serious adverse events (AEs) like myocarditis or Guillain-Barré syndrome. Pre-2026, most safety data come from passive reporting systems (e.g., VAERS) or limited cohort studies, which can lack comprehensive control for confounding and precise temporal analysis. Applying the same robust, large-scale methods used to study vaccine effectiveness to the dual problem of safety surveillance is a critical unmet need.
proposals.hypothesis: Using a self-controlled case series (SCCS) design within a large, integrated health system, we hypothesize that the incidence rates of specific, pre-specified cardiovascular and neurological events will be significantly elevated in defined risk intervals (e.g., 1-21 days) following mRNA COVID-19 vaccination compared to control periods, after adjusting for seasonality and SARS-CoV-2 infection status.
proposals.method: We will leverage the extensive electronic health record and claims data from a large U.S. healthcare system (e.g., Intermountain Health, Kaiser Permanente). The study population will include all individuals aged 12+ receiving mRNA COVID-19 vaccines. We will employ a self-controlled case series (SCCS) design, identifying individuals with incident diagnoses of pre-specified outcomes (e.g., myocarditis, pericarditis, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis). For each case, we will compare the event rate in pre-defined post-vaccination risk intervals (dose 1, dose 2, booster) to the rate in baseline control periods, using conditional Poisson regression. Analyses will be stratified by age, sex, and vaccine product, and will adjust for documented SARS-CoV-2 infection as a time-varying covariate.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-quality, real-world evidence on the absolute and relative risks of rare AEs, complementing existing vaccine effectiveness research from the same cohort. It will demonstrate a framework for active, near-real-time safety surveillance using routine healthcare data. The findings will directly inform clinical risk-benefit discussions and public health guidance.
proposals.novelty: This applies the sophisticated, confounder-controlled methods (like SCCS) and large-scale data infrastructure previously used by this researcher to prove vaccine *effectiveness* to the parallel challenge of vaccine *safety* signal detection, creating a unified methodological platform for benefit-risk assessment.

proposals.title: Global Variation in Waning of COVID-19 mRNA Vaccine Effectiveness Against Severe Disease: A Harmonized Test-Negative Design Analysis Across International Surveillance Networks
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, understanding the durability of COVID-19 vaccine protection is critical for informing booster policies. Prior studies, including Dr. Stenehjem's, show waning varies by strain and population, but comparisons across countries are confounded by heterogeneous study designs, data sources, and circulating variants. A harmonized global analysis is needed to disentangle regional differences in waning from methodological artifacts.
proposals.hypothesis: After adjusting for variant predominance and baseline risk, the rate of waning in mRNA vaccine effectiveness against COVID-19-associated hospitalization differs significantly across global regions, influenced by factors such as prior infection prevalence, vaccine product mix, and dosing intervals.
proposals.method: We will implement a harmonized test-negative design across established networks (e.g., VISION in the US, networks in the UK, Israel, and Canada). The core protocol will define consistent inclusion criteria (adults with COVID-like illness tested via PCR), outcomes (hospitalization within 14 days), exposure (vaccine dose dates and products), and confounder adjustment (age, comorbidities, calendar time). Waning will be modeled using a piecewise logistic regression with an interaction term between time-since-vaccination (in 30-day bins) and region, stratified by variant epoch (Delta, Omicron BA.1/BA.2, BA.4/BA.5).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first directly comparable estimates of vaccine waning across multiple countries, identifying regions with faster-than-expected waning that may need tailored booster schedules. It will establish a scalable framework for rapid, coordinated global vaccine surveillance for future pathogens.
proposals.novelty: While test-negative designs are common, no pre-2026 study has prospectively harmonized the protocol across major international consortia to isolate true regional differences in waning, moving beyond single-network or meta-analytic approaches that cannot fully adjust for methodological heterogeneity.

proposals.title: Waning of mRNA Vaccine Effectiveness Against SARS-CoV-2 Infection and Subsequent Transmission: A Test-Negative Design Study in a Large Integrated Health System
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence shows mRNA vaccine effectiveness (VE) against severe COVID-19 wanes over time, but protection against infection and transmission is less well characterized. Understanding this dual waning is critical for modeling epidemic dynamics and informing public health strategies, such as booster timing and non-pharmaceutical interventions, especially against Omicron subvariants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mRNA vaccine effectiveness against both documented SARS-CoV-2 infection (a prerequisite for transmission) and against secondary transmission from breakthrough cases wanes significantly within 6 months post-vaccination, with protection against transmission waning more rapidly than protection against infection.
proposals.method: We will apply a test-negative case-control design within the Kaiser Permanente system, leveraging linked EHR and vaccination data. For VE against infection, cases are PCR-positive individuals; controls are PCR-negative individuals with symptomatic encounters. To estimate VE against transmission, we will perform a secondary household contact study: among vaccinated, PCR-positive index cases, we will compare the secondary attack rates to those from unvaccinated index cases, adjusting for household size, age, and the contact's vaccination status using multivariable logistic regression. Time-since-vaccination will be modeled using splines.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide concurrent, population-based estimates of waning protection against both infection and downstream transmission, filling a key evidence gap. The results will directly inform transmission models and the rationale for booster vaccinations aimed at reducing community spread, not just severe disease.
proposals.novelty: While prior work estimates VE against infection or uses modeling to infer transmission effects, this proposal directly integrates a household transmission study within a large-scale VE framework to empirically estimate the vaccine's impact on reducing infectiousness.

proposals.title: Associations of mRNA Vaccination and Prior Infection with Post-Acute Sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 (PASC) and Viral Shedding Dynamics in a High-Risk Psychiatric Cohort
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Stenehjem's, has focused on COVID-19 hospitalization as a primary endpoint for vaccine effectiveness, especially in vulnerable groups like those with psychiatric disorders. However, given the high prevalence of Long COVID and the role of viral shedding in transmission, these outcomes may be more relevant to long-term patient morbidity and public health. A shift in focus is needed to evaluate interventions against these more nuanced, yet debilitating, consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: Among adults with psychiatric disorders, mRNA vaccination and prior SARS-CoV-2 infection are associated with a reduced risk of developing Long COVID (PASC) and a shorter duration of detectable viral RNA shedding following breakthrough infection, independent of protection against hospitalization.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked EHR and viral surveillance data from a large integrated health system (e.g., Intermountain Healthcare) from Delta through Omicron BA.5 predominance (pre-2026). The cohort will include adults with psychiatric diagnoses and confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection. We will define Long COVID using a validated computable phenotype based on ICD-10 codes and persistent symptoms documented in clinical encounters ≥28 days post-infection. Viral shedding duration will be assessed via longitudinal PCR cycle threshold (Ct) values from routine clinical testing. Using multivariable Cox proportional hazards and generalized estimating equation models, we will estimate adjusted hazard ratios for Long COVID and rate of viral RNA clearance, adjusting for vaccination status (primary series/booster, time since dose), prior infection history, demographics, and comorbidities.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first estimates of vaccine effectiveness against Long COVID and viral shedding dynamics specifically in a psychiatric population, a group at high risk for severe acute outcomes. It will inform clinical and public health strategies by identifying whether current vaccination paradigms offer protection beyond preventing hospitalization, addressing long-term disability and transmission risk.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by this researcher and others quantified protection against hospital encounters, this proposal innovatively applies similar rigorous, encounter-based methods to two understudied but critical endpoints: a clinically defined Long COVID syndrome and virologic clearance, moving beyond the traditional acute severe disease framework.

proposals.title: High-Dimensional Phenotyping of Immunocompromised Adults to Predict COVID-19 Vaccine Breakthrough Hospitalizations: A Machine Learning Analysis of Electronic Health Records
proposals.motivation: Immunocompromised individuals remain at disproportionate risk for severe COVID-19 outcomes despite vaccination, but this population is highly heterogeneous. Current risk stratification is often based on broad diagnostic categories, which may obscure critical subgroups at the highest risk of vaccine failure, leading to suboptimal targeting of prophylactic and early therapeutic interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning applied to high-dimensional clinical and pharmacoepidemiologic data can identify distinct, high-risk immunocompromised phenotypes with significantly elevated odds of COVID-19 hospitalization post-vaccination, beyond traditional classification schemes.
proposals.method: We will use a retrospective cohort design with data from a large US healthcare system (2019-2024). The outcome is a COVID-19-associated hospitalization ≥14 days after primary mRNA vaccination series. We will engineer features from EHRs including detailed immunosuppressive medication regimens (type, dose, duration), encounter histories, prior infection, vaccination dates/doses, antibiotic use, and comorbidities. We will train and compare multiple algorithms, including regularized logistic regression (LASSO), random forests, and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), to predict the outcome. The best-performing model will be interpreted using SHAP values to define high-risk clinical profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a clinically actionable, data-driven taxonomy of risk within the immunocompromised population, moving beyond binary definitions. It will provide evidence for tailored clinical guidelines and inform the design of trials for enhanced prophylactic strategies in the most vulnerable subgroups identified.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has established general risk in immunocompromised patients, this study uniquely integrates fine-grained treatment and temporal data (e.g., immunosuppressive dose trajectories) with advanced ML to discover novel, high-dimensional risk phenotypes, a method not yet widely applied in this specific context of COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness.

proposals.title: Effectiveness of mRNA Vaccination Against Symptomatic Outpatient SARS-CoV-2 Infection During Omicron Subvariant Circulation: A Test-Negative Design Study in a Large Integrated Health System
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, most real-world COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness (VE) studies using the test-negative design (TND) have focused on severe outcomes like hospitalization. VE against mild, outpatient SARS-CoV-2 infection, particularly during the Omicron era, is less characterized but critical for understanding population-level transmission dynamics and the full spectrum of vaccine protection. Adapting the robust TND framework to outpatient settings is essential for timely, accurate assessment of VE against circulating variants in community populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mRNA vaccination (primary series and boosters) provides significant but waning protection against symptomatic, laboratory-confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection among adults seeking outpatient care, and that this protection varies by vaccine product, number of doses, and time since last dose during periods of Omicron subvariant predominance.
proposals.method: We will employ a test-negative case-control study within Kaiser Permanente health systems. Cases are adult members with symptomatic illness who test positive for SARS-CoV-2 via PCR or antigen test in outpatient/ED settings. Controls are symptomatic members testing negative during the same period. We will use electronic health records to ascertain vaccination status (dose, dates, product), prior infections, and covariates (demographics, comorbidities, prior healthcare encounters). VE will be calculated as (1 – adjusted odds ratio) x 100% from multivariable logistic regression models, stratified by variant period (e.g., BA.1, BA.4/BA.5). We will specifically model waning using time-since-vaccination as a continuous variable.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, population-based estimates of mRNA VE against symptomatic outpatient infection, filling a key evidence gap. It will directly inform public health guidance on booster timing and expectations for infection-induced immunity in the community. The adapted methodological framework will serve as a model for rapid outpatient VE assessment for future respiratory pathogens.
proposals.novelty: While TND is established for hospital studies, its rigorous application to high-throughput outpatient testing data to estimate VE against mild breakthrough infections is less common. This proposal innovates by integrating detailed variant period stratification and waning analysis specifically for the outpatient setting, leveraging Dr. Stenehjem's expertise in large-scale encounter-based VE studies.

proposals.title: Leveraging Differential COVID-19 Vaccine Effectiveness to Identify Populations with Undiagnosed Immunocompromising Conditions: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies, including those by Stenehjem et al., have established that vaccine effectiveness (VE) against COVID-19 outcomes is significantly lower in clinically recognized immunocompromised populations. However, a substantial number of individuals with immunocompromising conditions may remain undiagnosed, leaving them vulnerable to severe outcomes despite vaccination. Identifying these populations is critical for targeted clinical interventions and public health strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by analyzing differential rates of vaccine failure (e.g., breakthrough hospitalizations) across demographic and clinical subgroups in large healthcare databases, we can identify clusters of patients whose observed VE is consistent with an undiagnosed immunocompromised status, independent of known diagnostic codes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using data from the Veterans Health Administration or similar large integrated health network, analyzing adults who completed a primary mRNA vaccine series. We will calculate adjusted VE against COVID-19-associated hospital encounters during Omicron predominance. Using Poisson regression models, we will identify residual patient subgroups (defined by combinations of demographics, chronic conditions, and medication use like antibiotics) with anomalously low VE. A machine learning model (gradient boosting) will then be trained on these VE signatures, along with healthcare utilization patterns (encounters, dose timing), to predict individuals at high risk for undiagnosed immunodeficiency, which will be validated against subsequent clinical diagnosis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven method for population health screening to identify patients at risk for impaired immunogenicity who are missed by current diagnostic practices. It will advance the field of vaccine epidemiology by inverting the traditional VE paradigm to become a diagnostic tool. The findings could inform clinical guidelines for targeted immune function testing and booster dose prioritization.
proposals.novelty: While prior work characterizes VE in known risk groups, this study innovatively uses population-level VE disparities as a signal to *discover* potential risk groups, treating vaccine failure patterns as an inverse problem to infer underlying immune status. This approach moves beyond descriptive epidemiology to predictive analytics for condition detection.

proposals.title: Evaluation of the COVID-19-associated Hyperinflammatory Syndrome (cHIS) Score for Guiding Immunomodulatory Therapy in Hospitalized Adults: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: By late 2025, immunomodulators like tocilizumab and baricitinib are established for severe COVID-19, but optimal patient selection remains challenging. The COVID-19-associated Hyperinflammatory Syndrome (cHIS) score, derived from routine lab values, identifies a high-inflammatory phenotype but has not been rigorously evaluated as a predictive biomarker to guide the initiation of specific immunomodulatory therapies in clinical practice.
proposals.hypothesis: Among hospitalized adults with COVID-19, initiation of immunomodulatory therapy guided by an elevated cHIS score (≥ 4) will be associated with improved clinical outcomes (e.g., reduced mortality, shorter time to clinical improvement) compared to treatment not guided by the score, after adjusting for vaccination status, prior infection, and other confounders.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from 2020-2025 on hospitalized adults with confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection. The primary exposure will be immunomodulator (tocilizumab or baricitinib) initiation within 48 hours of meeting cHIS criteria (score ≥4). The comparator will be patients meeting cHIS criteria who did not receive therapy. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards and logistic regression models, adjusting for days from symptom onset, vaccination dose/status, prior COVID-19 encounters, concurrent antibiotic/steroid use, and baseline comorbidities. Subgroup analyses will focus on Delta and Omicron variant periods.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first real-world evidence on whether the cHIS score can serve as a practical, objective tool to optimize immunomodulatory therapy decisions in COVID-19. It will directly inform clinical protocols for treatment escalation and could improve resource allocation and patient outcomes by targeting therapy to those most likely to benefit.
proposals.novelty: While cHIS is known to define a high-risk phenotype, its utility as a *treatment guidance rule* for specific immunomodulators, analogous to antibiotic stewardship algorithms for sepsis, is novel and untested in large-scale observational studies.

proposals.title: Post-Discharge Morbidity and Mortality Among COVID-19 Survivors with a Clinical Hyperinflammatory Syndrome (cHIS): A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 Clinical Hyperinflammatory Syndrome (cHIS) is a validated, pre-2026 clinical score identifying patients with a heightened systemic inflammatory response, a known driver of severe acute outcomes. However, the long-term trajectory of survivors who experienced this profound inflammatory state remains poorly characterized. Understanding whether cHIS portends a higher risk of post-acute sequelae, re-hospitalization, or mortality is critical for risk stratification and guiding post-discharge care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adult COVID-19 survivors who met cHIS criteria during their index hospitalization will have significantly higher rates of all-cause mortality, hospital readmission, and new chronic disease diagnoses in the 12 months post-discharge compared to matched COVID-19 survivors who did not meet cHIS criteria.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from a large, integrated health system (e.g., Veterans Health Administration or Kaiser Permanente). We will identify adults hospitalized with confirmed COVID-19 (positive PCR test) and calculate the cHIS score (based on temperature, hypotension, tachypnea, hypoxia, and specific lab values) from the first 72 hours of admission. The exposed cohort (cHIS-positive) will be 1:1 propensity-score matched to unexposed controls (cHIS-negative) on age, sex, vaccination status, comorbidities, and admission date. Primary outcomes will be time to all-cause mortality and time to first all-cause hospital readmission within 365 days of discharge, analyzed using Kaplan-Meier estimators and Cox proportional hazards models, adjusting for residual confounding.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence linking a validated, acute-phase inflammatory phenotype (cHIS) to long-term clinical outcomes in COVID-19. It will inform clinical guidelines by identifying a high-risk survivor population that may benefit from enhanced monitoring and intervention. The findings will also contribute to the broader understanding of how severe systemic inflammation during infection shapes recovery and chronic health.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Stenehjem and others has focused on acute protection from vaccination/infection and initial sepsis diagnosis, this study uniquely applies a specific, biologically grounded inflammatory syndrome score (cHIS) as a predictor of long-term post-COVID outcomes, moving beyond demographic or comorbidity-based risk models.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmacokinetic Bridging Strategies to Pediatric Vaccine Effectiveness
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from clinical pharmacology to the challenge of estimating vaccine effectiveness (VE) in pediatric and adolescent populations. In drug development, pharmacokinetic bridging studies allow for the extrapolation of adult efficacy data to pediatric groups when the disease and drug effects are similar, streamlining the approval process. By adapting this solution, the core VISION Network methodology—a robust test-negative design for VE estimation in adults—would serve as the validated reference framework. The research would then systematically identify and adjust for key pediatric-specific effect modifiers (like immune maturation, variant exposure, and vaccine dosing) to 'bridge' the estimates to younger age groups. This approach provides a structured, efficient pathway to generate reliable pediatric VE estimates by leveraging the existing adult observational infrastructure rather than building an entirely new study design from scratch.

ideas.title: Dynamic Utility-Optimized Booster Scheduling Under Waning Immunity and Variant Emergence
ideas.description: This research integrates utility theory from economics with longitudinal analysis of waning vaccine effectiveness to develop a decision-support model for public health policy. It formally assigns utilities (or disutilities) to outcomes like vaccine side effects, breakthrough infections, and severe disease, which vary based on an individual's time-since-vaccination and the prevailing variant landscape. The core innovation is a dynamic, utility-maximizing algorithm that recommends when and for whom booster doses provide the greatest net benefit, moving beyond static thresholds. This enables personalized, resource-efficient vaccination strategies that adapt to real-world evidence on immunity decay and viral evolution.

ideas.title: Disentangling Immunological Waning from Depletion of Susceptibles in Long-Term Vaccine Effectiveness
ideas.description: This study implements a multi-cohort, longitudinal design to rigorously separate true waning of vaccine-conferred immunity from the confounding effects of changing population susceptibility and exposure. Using a test-negative design with time-matched controls and continuous-time survival models, it tracks individuals from vaccination, adjusting for time-varying community incidence of specific variants and individual prior infection status. The analysis explicitly models the force of infection and the shrinking pool of susceptibles to produce unbiased estimates of how intrinsic immune protection decays over extended periods, independent of epidemiological context.

ideas.title: Model vaccine waning as a material fatigue process under variant stress cycles
ideas.description: This research idea applies the principles of material fatigue modeling from materials science to the problem of waning vaccine immunity. In the analogous field, the progressive failure of materials under cyclic loading is predicted using models like S-N curves and damage accumulation rules. The solution is adapted by treating the immune protection conferred by vaccination as the 'material' and each exposure to a circulating SARS-CoV-2 variant as a 'stress cycle' of a specific magnitude (based on the variant's antigenic distance). The research would involve analyzing longitudinal effectiveness data to fit a model that quantifies the cumulative 'damage' to vaccine efficacy over time, parameterized by the sequence and properties of the circulating variants. This approach moves beyond measuring simple time-since-vaccination effects to a mechanistic model that can forecast waning under specific, dynamic variant pressures.

ideas.title: Real-time, Variant-Adaptive Vaccine Effectiveness and Clinical Risk Prediction using Dynamic Ensemble Learning
ideas.description: This research integrates dynamic VE monitoring with ensemble machine learning for individual risk prediction. It develops a framework where real-world VE data against newly predominant variants are continuously fed as a key, time-varying feature into ensemble models that also incorporate patient demographics, comorbidities, and behavioral factors. This allows the models to generate dynamic, personalized risk scores for breakthrough infection and severe outcomes that automatically recalibrate as the virus evolves and population immunity shifts. The synergy enables a closed-loop system where population-level surveillance directly informs and refines individual-level clinical risk assessments in near real-time.

ideas.title: Immune State Phenotyping and Quantitative Bias Adjustment for Causal Inference
ideas.description: This study moves beyond simple adjustment for prior infection by first rigorously defining and measuring an individual's composite immune state through a multi-modal assay protocol. It combines serology for infection-derived antibodies, T-cell assays, and documented vaccination history to create a high-fidelity, multi-category immune status variable. This precisely measured confounder is then incorporated into the primary analytical model. Crucially, a second-stage quantitative bias analysis is performed to model the impact of any residual misclassification, producing effect estimates with explicit uncertainty bounds that account for measurement error.

ideas.title: Dynamic Risk-Adaptive Stewardship: Integrating Real-Time Diagnostics with Patient-Specific Harm Prediction to Guide Antibiotic Initiation and De-escalation
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a clinical decision support system that integrates two sequential data streams. First, it uses a model to identify patients at high predicted risk of harm (e.g., C. difficile, resistance, adverse events) from unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotics at the moment of empiric therapy consideration. For these high-risk patients, the system then triggers a prioritized, protocol-driven rapid diagnostic pathway (e.g., multiplex PCR, mass spectrometry) with explicit time targets, creating a 'diagnostic hold' alternative to immediate broad-spectrum initiation when clinically safe. The synergy lies in using the harm prediction to risk-stratify which patients benefit most from a rapid diagnostic-driven delay, thereby safely relaxing the need for immediate empiric coverage specifically in the cohort where it is most dangerous, and using the diagnostic result to confirm the prediction and guide precise de-escalation or targeted escalation.

ideas.title: Spatial Phylogenetic Analysis of Regional Variant Impact on Vaccine Efficacy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of uniform Delta variant impact by adapting methods from ecology and evolutionary biology. In ecology, the problem of assessing local adaptation is solved by spatially mapping traits and using phylogenetic comparative methods to separate ancestry from environmental adaptation. Adapted here, the research would treat U.S. states as distinct environments. By constructing state-specific phylogenetic trees from sequenced SARS-CoV-2 samples (focusing on Delta sub-lineages and co-circulating strains) and correlating phylogenetic metrics (e.g., genetic distance, clade structure) with state-level vaccine effectiveness (VE) estimates, we can test whether regional VE differences are better explained by local viral phylogeny—suggesting a true variant-driven effect—rather than by demographic or policy-based state covariates alone.

ideas.title: Geospatially-Informed, Ultra-Simple Triage (GIUST) Score for Drug-Resistant Pneumonia
ideas.description: This research develops a two-tiered clinical decision support system. First, geospatial analysis techniques are used to create high-resolution, community-level risk maps for drug-resistant pneumonia, identifying hotspots based on epidemiological and environmental data. Second, this geographic risk data is integrated into the development and application of an ultra-simple clinical score, designed for emergency triage with near-perfect sensitivity. The synergy is achieved by having the geospatial risk stratum (e.g., high-risk ZIP code) determine whether the minimal score is applied in its most stringent form or if it is relaxed, ensuring the 'rule-out' function remains safe across diverse community contexts without adding complexity for the frontline clinician.

ideas.title: Network-Aware, Exposure-Adjusted Antibiogram for Empiric Therapy
ideas.description: This system generates empiric therapy guidelines by primarily using the local facility antibiogram, but dynamically adjusts them for individual patients based on recent healthcare exposures. It operationalizes regional healthcare network data to identify patients with recent hospitalization or long-term care facility stays outside the local catchment. For these patients, it creates a blended resistance risk estimate incorporating the epidemiology of the likely source facility, ensuring guidelines are robust against imported resistance. This maintains the simplicity of population-level rules while correcting their most dangerous blind spot.

ideas.title: Adapting Geological Stratigraphy to Isolate the Signal of Natural Immunity
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that prior infection status is a negligible or simple binary confounder by adapting a solution from geology/stratigraphy. In stratigraphy, unique chemical or fossil markers are used to isolate the signal of a specific past event from overlapping background processes. The adapted research idea is to develop high-specificity immunological assays that identify definitive, durable signatures of past SARS-CoV-2 infection (e.g., unique non-spike antibody profiles, T-cell clonal expansions, or mucosal IgA patterns). These biomarkers would act as discrete 'event layers,' allowing researchers to precisely stratify study cohorts and quantitatively adjust for the confounding effect of natural immunity, separating its impact from that of vaccination and other forms of cross-reactive immunity in observational studies of vaccine effectiveness, transmission, or disease severity.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Context-Aware Safety Verification for Antibiotic De-escalation
ideas.description: This system moves beyond static risk scores by continuously integrating real-time local and regional epidemiological data to define 'safety' for avoiding broad-spectrum antibiotics. It employs a hybrid model that assesses individual patient factors against a dynamically updated map of circulating drug-resistant organisms and their prevalence in the specific hospital unit, region, and patient transfer network. This allows the protocol to adaptively tighten or loosen criteria for safe antibiotic narrowing based on proven, contemporaneous threat levels, preventing the application of outdated 'low-risk' labels during silent outbreaks or resistance shifts. The result is a resilient, self-correcting safety check that protects both the individual patient and the broader community from the unintended consequences of guideline misapplication in a changing microbial environment.

ideas.title: Adapting Anticipatory Failure Determination to Proactively Map Vaccine-Enhanced Disease Pathways
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from engineering systems safety, specifically Anticipatory Failure Determination (AFD), to the challenge of finding signals of vaccine-enhanced disease. In engineering, to prevent catastrophic failures, teams systematically invert the success condition to ask 'How could this system fail?' and then design tests for those specific hypothetical failures. The adapted research idea applies this structured 'inversion' methodology to vaccinology. Researchers would first proactively construct a comprehensive map of hypothetical biological and immunological pathways that could theoretically lead to enhanced disease (e.g., specific mechanisms of antibody-dependent enhancement, Th2-skewed immunopathology, or altered viral clearance dynamics). This map of potential 'failure modes' at the molecular and cellular level then directs a targeted, mechanism-based search for their specific signatures in existing immunological datasets, animal challenge studies, and clinical trial outcomes, moving beyond broad surveillance to a hypothesis-driven investigation of the worst-case immunological scenarios.

ideas.title: Continuous syndromic surveillance for pre-outbreak detection of drug-resistant pneumonia in long-term care
ideas.description: This system implements a continuous, facility-specific surveillance platform that analyzes routine electronic data streams—including antimicrobial orders, respiratory medication use, nursing documentation keywords, and vital sign trends—to detect anomalies indicative of emerging drug-resistant pneumonia clusters. It employs adaptive, facility-baselined statistical process control charts to flag deviations from expected patterns, triggering targeted diagnostic testing and infection control audits before an outbreak is clinically suspected. The approach moves the intervention point earlier in the outbreak curve, transforming the model from a reactive prediction tool into a proactive prevention system.

ideas.title: Integrated Diagnostic-Clinical Protocol for Time-Delimited Antimicrobial De-escalation
ideas.description: This research designs and validates a clinical management protocol that formally integrates rapid diagnostic test results with a parallel clinical prediction rule within a strict time frame. The protocol begins with standard empiric therapy but mandates a structured reassessment at 24-48 hours, where microbiological data and early clinical response are synthesized into a combined de-escalation decision score. This system ensures a clear pathway for action even when diagnostics are pending, uninformative, or negative, eliminating diagnostic paralysis. The outcome measure is the proportion of patients successfully de-escalated without harm, compared to both standard empiric therapy and diagnostic-guided approaches alone.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Hazard 'Alert Threshold' Models for Antibiotic Stewardship
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to a core problem in seismology—optimizing alert thresholds for earthquake early warning systems—to the clinical challenge of minimizing unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic use while maintaining high sensitivity for drug-resistant pathogens. In seismology, probabilistic models integrate multiple real-time data streams to calculate the likelihood of damaging shaking, and a public alert threshold is set by balancing the utility of true warnings against the cost of false alarms. The adapted idea is to develop a clinical prediction score that functions similarly: it would integrate multiple real-time, patient-specific data streams (e.g., host biomarkers, local epidemiology, clinical presentation) to generate a continuous probability of a drug-resistant pathogen. The innovation lies in explicitly defining and optimizing an 'antibiotic escalation threshold' for this probability score. The threshold would be calibrated not just for statistical performance but for clinical utility, formally weighing the consequences of a missed resistant infection (a false negative) against the consequences of unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure (a false positive). This approach moves beyond standard predictive modeling to create a decision tool explicitly engineered for antibiotic stewardship, analogous to how seismic alerts are engineered for public safety and economic impact.

ideas.title: Causal Geospatial Inference for Drug-Resistant Pneumonia Risk Attribution
ideas.description: This research develops a two-stage analytical framework to move beyond correlative risk mapping toward causal attribution of community-level risk for drug-resistant pneumonia. First, it applies spatial scan statistics and machine learning clustering to multi-source data (environmental, socioeconomic, healthcare infrastructure) to identify high-risk geographic areas. Second, it implements a targeted causal inference analysis within these clusters using patient-level electronic health records and antibiotic prescription data to isolate the specific community and environmental factors driving resistance, controlling for healthcare access and prescribing behavior. This approach generates robust, actionable risk maps that distinguish true community risk from healthcare system artifacts, enabling precisely targeted public health interventions.

ideas.title: Apply Mendelian Randomization from Genetic Epidemiology to Vaccine Effectiveness Studies
ideas.description: This idea adapts a causal inference solution from genetic epidemiology—Mendelian Randomization—to the problem of strengthening causal claims in vaccine effectiveness (VE) research. In its original field, Mendelian Randomization uses genetic variants as instrumental variables to infer causality between exposures and outcomes, leveraging the random assortment of genes at conception to minimize confounding. The adapted research idea proposes identifying a suitable genetic instrumental variable (e.g., a variant associated with propensity for vaccination) and applying this framework to observational health data. This would create a natural experiment to estimate the causal effect of vaccination, providing a robust methodological parallel to using instrumental variables in economics for VE analysis.

ideas.title: Pragmatic stepped-wedge trial with silent control to isolate the causal effect of prediction score-guided therapy
ideas.description: This redesigned study uses a cluster-randomized, stepped-wedge design where all patient episodes have novel prediction scores calculated in the background from the start. During initial 'silent control' phases, clinicians treat patients using standard guidance (e.g., HCAP) without access to these scores. At randomized steps, clusters cross over to the intervention phase where the scores are integrated into the electronic health record and guide therapy. The primary analysis compares outcomes for patients treated by the same clinical teams and sites during their own control versus intervention periods, rigorously controlling for secular trends and behavioral adaptation. This design isolates the causal effect of the predictive information itself on patient outcomes.

ideas.title: Distributed, Real-Time Outbreak Intelligence Network for Antimicrobial Resistance
ideas.description: This research proposes a federated learning network that integrates real-time, facility-level outbreak data from long-term care facilities into a continuously updated national prediction model for drug-resistant pneumonia. The system enables individual facilities to run localized outbreak prediction models that are dynamically calibrated with the latest national and regional resistance patterns, while anonymized, aggregated facility data strengthens the central model. This creates a virtuous cycle where local outbreak responses generate high-resolution temporal and spatial data, improving the accuracy and speed of predictions for all connected facilities. The result is a scalable, privacy-preserving early warning and decision-support system that evolves with the changing landscape of antibiotic resistance.

ideas.title: Local Ecological Niche Modeling for Antibiotic Selection
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that complex individual patient risk scores are always superior for guiding antibiotic therapy. By drawing an analogy from community ecology, where local environmental factors (like soil and climate) often trump individual organism traits in predicting species distribution, we adapt the solution of Species Distribution Models (SDMs). The adapted research idea is to prioritize developing dynamic, local 'antimicrobial resistance niche maps.' These maps would synthesize high-resolution, real-time local data—such as ward-specific antibiograms, unit-level antibiotic consumption, seasonal admission patterns, and local hospital microbiome profiles—into simple, visual, location-driven prescribing guidelines. This approach assumes that the local epidemiological 'environment' of a hospital ward or region is the dominant factor shaping resistance risk for a given infection, offering a more pragmatic and immediately actionable tool for clinicians than complex individual risk algorithms.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Aftershock Forecasting for Pneumonia Outbreak Prediction
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from seismology—specifically the Epidemic-Type Aftershock Sequence (ETAS) model used for forecasting aftershocks—to the problem of scaling prediction models for drug-resistant pneumonia outbreaks in long-term care facilities. The analogous solution uses statistical scaling laws in a self-exciting point process to forecast localized, cascading events following a primary shock. The adapted idea applies this framework by modeling initial pneumonia cases as triggering events, with parameters scaled to represent facility-specific pathogen transmissibility and resident interaction networks, thereby generating real-time, facility-level forecasts of outbreak magnitude and progression to guide infection control responses.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Continuously Validated Adaptive Rule-Out Protocol (AROP) for Drug-Resistant Pneumonia
ideas.description: This research develops an Adaptive Rule-Out Protocol (AROP) that maintains a core minimal triage checklist but embeds it within a dynamic validation system. The system continuously monitors the checklist's sensitivity in real-world use across multiple sites, tracking performance against local microbiological resistance patterns and patient demographics. If sensitivity drops below a pre-set safety threshold for any subpopulation, the system triggers an alert and a pre-validated, context-specific backup rule is automatically recommended, ensuring near-perfect sensitivity is adaptively preserved.

ideas.title: Use pharmacoepidemiologic active surveillance methods

ideas.title: Bayesian Adaptive Platform Trials for Dynamic, Locally-Optimized Empiric Therapy Guidelines
ideas.description: This research proposes a multi-center, Bayesian adaptive platform trial to dynamically generate and validate local empiric antibiotic guidelines. The trial would efficiently compare multiple, locally-tailored prediction rules (prioritizing local antibiogram data over complex patient scores) against a common control arm within a single, ongoing study. Using response-adaptive randomization, patient enrollment is steered toward the most promising local rule for each participating site or region in real-time. This creates a continuous feedback loop where local epidemiological data directly informs the trial's adaptive design, leading to the rapid, evidence-based optimization of antibiotic stewardship policies specific to each participating institution's microbial ecology.

ideas.title: Integrate multi-omic biomarker panels using ensemble machine learning for pneumonia resistance prediction
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution from financial fraud and credit risk modeling, where ensemble machine learning integrates traditional and novel, high-frequency data streams. The analogous field solves the problem of predicting rare, high-cost events (fraud/loan default) by combining stable historical factors with dynamic, real-time behavioral biomarkers in a weighted, algorithmic model. Adapted to infectious diseases, the idea is to build a clinical prediction tool for drug-resistant pneumonia that uses an ensemble model (e.g., random forest, XGBoost, or neural network) to synthesize static clinical risk factors with dynamic, multi-omic biomarker panels measured at presentation. This integrated model would be designed to capture complex interactions, provide a probabilistic risk score, and improve upon models using either data type alone, ultimately guiding more precise empiric antibiotic therapy.

ideas.title: Predicting Individual Collateral Damage from Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics Using Ecological Stability Metrics
ideas.description: This research inverts the antibiotic stewardship model by adapting a solution from theoretical ecology. The analogous problem is predicting which specific ecosystems will suffer catastrophic collapse from an introduced perturbation, solved by analyzing network stability and resilience metrics. The adapted idea applies this framework to the human microbiome to predict which patients will suffer harm from unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotics. By modeling the patient's gut microbiome as an ecological network and using pre-treatment metagenomic and host data to compute a personalized Microbiome Stability Index (MSI), the research aims to identify individuals at high risk for dysbiosis-driven adverse outcomes. This allows for a precision stewardship approach, where the 'dual problem' is solved by proactively avoiding antibiotics likely to cause the greatest collateral damage to a vulnerable patient's microbial ecology.

ideas.title: Pediatric CAP-DRP Model via Ecological Allometric Scaling
ideas.description: This research adapts the ecological solution of allometric scaling to create a pediatric-specific risk prediction model for community-acquired pneumonia drug-resistant pathogens (CAP-DRP). In ecology, allometric scaling laws (Y = aM^b) predict biological variables across species of different sizes by applying non-linear, size-dependent exponents. Analogously, instead of simply scaling down adult CAP-DRP model coefficients, we will identify key physiological and laboratory predictors (e.g., creatinine, white blood cell count) and derive pediatric-specific scaling exponents based on established developmental allometry and age-dependent reference ranges. The adult model provides the structural framework (the variables and their relationships), but each parameter is transformed using these developmentally-informed scaling laws. This results in a risk prediction tool that is fundamentally adapted to the non-linear physiological and immunological landscape of the pediatric population, moving beyond a simple linear reduction of an adult model.

ideas.title: Adapting Real-Time Structural Monitoring to Guide Antibiotic De-escalation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that empiric broad-spectrum coverage must be maintained until traditional, slower culture results return. Drawing an analogy from structural engineering's shift from static worst-case design to dynamic monitoring, we propose a similar paradigm shift in managing infections. In engineering, real-time sensor data guides targeted maintenance; here, a clinical 'sensor network' of rapid diagnostics provides real-time microbiological data. The adapted research idea is to develop and evaluate a protocol where therapy is not just initiated empirically but is actively and rapidly de-escalated within a critical early window (e.g., 3-6 hours) based on point-of-care test results, moving from a static, prediction-based model to an adaptive, evidence-based one to improve antibiotic stewardship and patient outcomes.

ideas.title: Standardized Benchmarking of Clinical Prediction Scores Using a Multi-Center Validation Framework
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from computational linguistics, where new algorithms are benchmarked on standardized datasets. The analogous problem is the need to objectively compare the performance of multiple competing models (here, clinical prediction scores) across diverse conditions. The solution involves creating a consortium-led, multi-center study that functions as a clinical benchmarking trial. A pre-defined, common protocol would be used at all participating centers to apply all nine identified prediction scores to prospectively enrolled, diverse patient populations. The core adaptation is the shift from conducting separate, potentially incomparable validation studies to a coordinated, head-to-head evaluation on identical data partitions across sites, generating a definitive, fair comparison of score performance in various real-world clinical environments, much like an NLP model leaderboard.

ideas.title: Interpretable Dynamic Risk Prediction via Explainable Ensemble Modeling and Counterfactual Narratives
ideas.description: This system first employs an ensemble machine learning model to achieve high-accuracy dynamic risk prediction. Its core innovation is a mandatory explanation module that deconstructs each prediction to identify the dominant risk factors and their interactions for that specific patient. Using counterfactual analysis, it then generates simple, actionable narratives (e.g., 'Your risk is elevated primarily due to the combined effect of X and Y; reducing Y to a specific target would lower your risk by Z%'). This ensures the model's complexity is translated into transparent, clinically useful guidance, fostering trust and facilitating personalized care plans.

ideas.title: Clinical Pathway Net Benefit Analysis: Integrating Decision Curves with Resource-Constrained Sequential Decision Modeling
ideas.description: This research develops a framework that extends decision curve analysis to evaluate prediction models within simulated clinical pathways. Instead of assessing net benefit at a single decision threshold, the system models sequences of diagnostic and treatment decisions, incorporating costs, harms, and resource constraints at each step. This allows for the comparison of how different prediction models influence the net benefit of entire care protocols under realistic clinical and operational conditions. The output identifies not just an optimal probability threshold, but the optimal integrated strategy for deploying a model within a constrained healthcare system.

ideas.title: Applying Seismic Microzonation Principles to Antibiotic Stewardship
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from earthquake engineering known as seismic microzonation, where detailed geological mapping identifies localized areas of stable ground suitable for less intensive building codes, thereby avoiding unnecessary over-engineering. The analogous problem in medicine is the blanket use of broad-spectrum antibiotics for all patients suspected of infection due to the fear of drug-resistant organisms. The adapted research idea is to invert the focus by defining clinical 'safety microzonation' criteria. This involves using multi-dimensional patient data—including specific biomarkers (e.g., low procalcitonin), absence of key risk factors, and knowledge of local community resistance prevalence—to map patients into verifiable 'low-risk safety zones' for drug-resistant organisms. Patients within these zones can then safely avoid empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics, receiving targeted therapy instead, just as buildings in seismically stable zones can safely use simpler, cost-effective designs.

ideas.title: Multi-Center Utility-Calibrated Validation of Clinical Prediction Models
ideas.description: This research conducts multi-center external validation of the nine prediction scores, but integrates utility theory directly into the validation paradigm. At each participating center, we will formally elicit from clinical stakeholders the local utilities (or disutilities) associated with overtreatment and undertreatment errors. This allows the validation to report not only traditional performance metrics (e.g., AUC, calibration) but also to calculate the utility-maximizing classification threshold for each model within each specific clinical and economic context. The outcome is a comparative analysis of which model delivers the highest net utility across diverse settings, providing directly actionable guidance for context-sensitive model implementation rather than a simple performance ranking.
